coredata.dc:title: Recent advances in MXenes: new horizons in biomedical technologies

coredata.dc:description: Biomedicine and nanotechnology are advancing at a breakneck speed resulted in an abundance of novel nanomaterials (NMs) based biomedical platforms. The 2D layered nano-structure material with discrete surface terminations and characteristics has shown enormous promise in diverse biological research, e.g. drug delivery, cancer treatments etc., that is the hot subject of recent era. Because of their nanometer thin two-dimensional (2D) planar morphology, highly effective specific surface area (SSA), plenteous functional groups on the surface, and novel physico-chemical properties, there is an increasing attention in investigating the possibilities of the MXenes family at the interface with living systems. MXene-based NMs can be employed as anticancer therapies, drug delivery platforms in photothermal therapy (PTT), or as nano-drugs without further modification. In this review, we will review current updates and potential of the MXenes in the biomedical applications. Therefore, we will review the recent developments, with a particular emphasis on cancer therapeutic/theranostics, biosensing, bioimaging, and antimicrobial therapies and finally, we will summarize the biocompatibility of MXenes NMs with their some limitations and future required advancements.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Biomedicine and nanotechnology are advancing at a breakneck speed resulted in an abundance of novel nanomaterials (NMs) based biomedical platforms. The 2D layered nano-structure material with discrete surface terminations and characteristics has shown enormous promise in diverse biological research, e.g. drug delivery, cancer treatments etc., that is the hot subject of recent era. Because of their nanometer thin two-dimensional (2D) planar morphology, highly effective specific surface area (SSA), plenteous functional groups on the surface, and novel physico-chemical properties, there is an increasing attention in investigating the possibilities of the MXenes family at the interface with living systems. MXene-based NMs can be employed as anticancer therapies, drug delivery platforms in photothermal therapy (PTT), or as nano-drugs without further modification. In this review, we will review current updates and potential of the MXenes in the biomedical applications. Therefore, we will review the recent developments, with a particular emphasis on cancer therapeutic/theranostics, biosensing, bioimaging, and antimicrobial therapies and finally, we will summarize the biocompatibility of MXenes NMs with their some limitations and future required advancements.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Biomedical applications of two dimensional (2D) materials have received various consideration and interdisciplinary research as a result of their distinctive planar nanostructure and outstanding physiochemical properties [1–5]. On account of their good biocompatibility and regulated biocompatibility, mechanical properties, ceramic-based biomaterials are commonly used as bone scaffolds in the hard tissue engineering; however their potential biomedical applications in disease theranostics fewer people paying attention, owing to their material functionalities that aren't related for having the potential to enter and circulate inside the circulatory system [6].

The insight that 2D compounds do not always necessitate van der Waals (vdWs) connected layered precursors lead to the innovation of a plethora of new nanomaterials (NMs), e.g. MXenes; 2D TMs carbides/nitrides/carbonitrides [3–5],[7–61]. MXenes, a new class of atomic sized nanosheets (NSs) ceramic materials mostly derived from the MAX phases [62], but with few recently from non-MAX phase as well as from bottom-up methods [63], are gaining traction as inorganic nano-systems for biological and biomedical applications [57,64]. MXenes are a kind of 2D inorganic complex made up of transition metal (TM) carbides (TMCs), TM nitrides (TMNs), or TM carbonitrides (TMCNs) with only a single or few atomic level of thickness. Initially, to produce MXene, mainly a single or different combination of acids based solution were employed to etch ‘A’-elements (usually Al and Ga) selectively from layered ternary carbide or nitride (MAX phase, where M corresponds to a TMs and X denotes carbon and/or nitrogen as a precursor.

The MXenes ‘gold rush’ officially began in 2017. Since then, family of 2D TMCs and TMNs has grown at astonishing rate. Recently employs bottom-up techniques, like Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD) etc method used to synthesize MXenes to get highly crystalline MXenes NMs. The Mn+1XnTz (n = 1–4) is a general formula for the MXenes, where ‘M’ is early TMs (M = Sc, Ti, Mo, Zr, Cr, Ta, Hf, V, Nb, and so on), X is carbon and/or nitrogen, and Tz is surface termination [10]. MXenes have been shown abundant surface single or mixed terminations/functional groups, for instance, O, F, Cl, Br, S, Se, Te, OH, NH, etc, reliant on the synthesis route applied and MXene composition and hence their properties as well as devices applications strongly depend on it [65]. MXenes have the M2XTz, M3X2Tz, M4X3Tz and M5X4Tz typical structures, where n-layer X element is covered by n+1 layer M. Additional more than 50-types of MXenes [65] have been experimentally studied since the breakthrough of MXene Ti3C2Tz in 2011, and many more MXenes which are theoretically investigated are also expected to experimentally investigate in near future [66]. Later, M5C4 was created, adding structural variety and causing the total number of theoretically conceivable compositions to over 100, including those with in-plane (in-MXenes) and out-of-plane (o-MXenes) the metal atom ordering. The number of unique compositions grows by an order of magnitude when different surface terminations of MXenes are considered. MXenes' propensity to produce carbonitrides and solid solutions indicates an endless number of compositions, ushering in a recent age of theoretically studied atomistic design of 2D NMs (Fig. 1 ) [65].

MXene has a number of outstanding electrical, optical, and magnetic characteristics. MXene has a greatest metallic conductivity of any 2D material that has been produced (maximum achieved conductivity of 20,000Scm−1) [69]. For example, SL titanium carbide MXene (Ti3C2Tz), the most frequently researched component has electrical conductivity of about 10,000 S.cm−1 [70,71], whereas in comparison higher than a graphene oxide (GO) conductivity, which is about 3,000 S.cm−1 [71,72]. Emission of MXenes is mostly owing to size factors, such as quantum confinement and defects, in terms of luminescence phenomena. Because of its highly organized structure, the nature of the MXenes may be anticipated using theoretical calculations. The MXenes applications have extended in numerous directions like, electrocatalysis, absorption, energy storage/production, electromagnetic interference (EMI) shielding, sensing applications etc., relayed on their properties and hydrophilicity combined with superior dispersion stability. Also, because of their metallic conductivity, hydrophilic nature, and other novel physiochemical properties, MXenes NSs can somehow achieved stringent demands of biomedical applications, especially in biomedicine.

In this paper we will discuss the recent progress, synthesis approaches in the MXenes and novel paradigms for cutting-edge biomedical applications, with an emphasis on schemes/production approaches like, anticancer therapy, bioimaging, therapeutic modalities, theranostics, bio-sensing, bio-safety/cytotoxicity estimations, contrast-enhanced diagnostic imaging, anti-microbial, and bio-safety issues applications. The intricately engineered ultrathin MXenes NSs are expected to become one of mainly appealing biocompatible inorganic nanoplatforms for a variety of bio-medical applications, aiding in the clinical translation of nanomedicine.

MXenes are synthesized by range of techniques can be mainly categorized as, bottom-up and top-down processes. The right method is critical for establishing the material's physical and chemical characteristics, for instance, size, form, defects, doping, functioning etc., [73]. Now we will discuss and summarized the different MXenes synthesis methods in the coming section.

Top-down production, which begins with the MAX phase acid based exfoliation of stacked ‘A’ solids, which is the first and favored approach for fabrication of MXene NSs but with some limitations in different applications (Fig. 2 (a)). As MXene's layered structure is similar to that of its 3D relative [74], this process is regarded as conventional. Two primary processes are the etching or cleavage of MAX based NMs and delamination of exfoliated MXene layers. To synthesize MAX phase, pure powders of A, M, and X in certain atomic ratios were combined and annealed at extremely critical temperatures conditions (1200–1600 °C) [75]. Combined materials will next be hot/cold pressed to densified material and remove any micro-voids or cracks [76]. The grain growth direction is determined by the pressure exerted during the hot/cold pressing (25–45 MPa). One disadvantage of this process was that it necessitates a large amount of ‘A’-element powder (e.g. Si or Al), which is quickly evaporated and absorbed into inert-gas or vacuum environment at elevated temperatures [77]. An additional typical approach of MAX phase synthesis is pressure-free sintering, which involves merely heating the mixed MAX powders to a certain temperature. In comparison to the hot-pressed Ti3AlC2, pressure-free sintering has been shown to yield highly orientated Ti3AlC2 MAX phase [78]. However, sintering restricts the final MAX phase solids' growth direction. After MAX phase is synthesized, initial step is to etch 3D MAX phase, for instance, Ti3AlC2, with a strong etchant, typically HF [79]. Due to the fact that the M-A bonds are weak as compared to the M-X bonds so, selective etching of M-A bonds is conceivable. As F-ions bind strongly to A-element (or Al), significant concentrations of F are required to etch A-element from a shared MAX phase [80]. Equations (1)–(3) depict a typical chemical interactions of MAX phase (M3AX2) with HF (Al was used as A-layer).
(1) Mn+1AlXn(s) + 3HF(aq) → Mn+1Xn(s) + AlF3(aq) + 3/2H2(g)
(2) Mn+1Xn(s) + 2HF(aq) → Mn+1XnF2(s) + 2H2(g)
(3) Mn+1Xn(s) + 2H2O(aq) → Mn+1Xn(OH)2(s) + H2(g).

In addition, Fig. 2(b) shows timelines of MXenes progress since his discovery (2011) to present [81]. The HF based etching of MAX phase caused a distinct Tz of OH, O, and F groups, respectively. Previous investigations studied HF-etching of the MAX phases utilizing various HF concentrations, immersion durations, and temperatures. Naguib et al. [79], created the first MXene by soaking Ti3AlC2 powders in 50-percent concentrated HF at room temperature (RT) for 2 hs to achieve complete dissociation of MAX phase. Also, Ti3C2 MXene formation with 48-percent HF–H2O stirred at RT for 24 hs [82] and Ti3C2 ML NSs synthesis with 40-percent HF stirred at 450 °C for 2-days [83]. An additional extensively applied etching approach is the in-situ HF production, which occurs when acid (e.g. HCl) reacts with a fluoride salt when HF is mixed [84]. This method provides for a safer aluminum etching while avoiding the detrimental effects of the HF concentration.

First research investigation were done on fluoride salt etching of MAX based materials to employ LiF and HCl solution heated to 40 °C for 45 hs [85]. This method was also used to successfully manufacture other MAX NMs, for instance, the Nb2AlC and Ti2AlC, showing that it is flexible to a wide range of the MAX phases. Various fluoride salts, including as LiF, NaF, KF, and NH4F, may also be etched in HCl at the proper temperature to create Ti3C2 and Ti2C NSs, ensuing in MXene that can store and absorb CH4 at high pressure [86]. Another work used simply NH4HF2 to etch Ti3AlC2 NSs without the use of strong acids [80]. They discovered that the MXene layers were concurrently intercalated with NH4+ during the etching process, resulting in layers with a higher lattice parameter (>25%) than the HF-etched Ti3C2. Even though several researches continue to utilize the HF/fluoride as a prefer MAX phase etchant, but HF is very toxic and unsafe in handling, particularly in biological uses, where even a little quantity of un-reacted HF can cause cell death [74]. The HF may produce systemic toxicity in the humans, which can be lethal. As a result, direct HF usage, or even in-situ HF synthesis, causes security and ecological risks, impeding development of MXenes [87]. The excess of F on MXene plane that reduces numerous other functional groups is another disadvantage of this form of etching (OH, O). It is easy to functionalize O and OH functional groups, but conjugating F-ions is difficult [74]. As a result, a fluorine-free etching approach is better for producing MXenes with regulated functional surface termination for biomedical uses. Li et al. [88], suggested and effectively produced the MLs Ti3C2Tx utilizing alkali-assisted hydro-thermal process by means of aqueous NaOH solution as etchant at 270 °C. They reported producing excellence Ti3C2 powder with 92-percent purity and more OH and O terminations using this procedure.

A different approach uses molten salt, which is made up by combining Ti4AlN3 powder with molten fluoride salt (a particular ratio of LiF, KF, and NaF) at 550 °C for 30 min [89]. The MLs and SLs MXenes were generated by delamination of the Ti4N3 layers with the Tetrabutylammonium hydroxide (TBAOH). MXenes are generally delaminated after etching to separate MXene NSs and examine their characteristics. Intercalating agent/intercalant and sonication steps can be used to delaminate the MXenes, with the lack of intercalating agents yielding smaller MXenes with bigger flaws. Intercalating chemicals frequently enhance c-lattice parameter, or spacing in two successive the MXene NSs, manufacturing it simpler to separate/delaminate them into pristine MXene NSs. The iso-propylamine and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), both polar organic solvents are examples of frequent intercalants [90,91] (Table 1 ).

The c-lattice value increased from 19.5 0.1 to 35.04 0.02 after delamination of Ti3C2 NSs with DMSO for 24 hs at RT, showing that the MXene NSs were effectively intercalated with DMSO [98]. Interlayer distance enlarged by 12.3% when Nb2C MXenes for lithium ions batteries (LIBs) were delaminated for 18 hs at RT using isopropylamine as the intercalant [99]. Intercalants such as TBAOH and TPAOH have also been found [100]. When TBAOH was used to delaminate V2CTx and Ti3CNTx, it caused massive spontaneous swelling while simultaneously weakening interlayer contacts, resulting in higher yields of delaminated MXene than when DMSO was employed [100]. The HF etching for 720 hs and then intercalation with the TPAOH same time was used to synthesize an ultrathin Ti3C2 nanoflakes (NFs) (5–6 nm) utilizing a modified chemical exfoliation [101]. The thin Ti3C2 NFs had an average lateral size of roughly 150 nm, and when intercalated with TPAOH, the NFs took on 2D NSs-like shape. For biological applications, the MXene NSs must be nanoscale in size. Large NSs are produced by traditional ML MXenes synthesized from the top-down technique, which might lead to bio-safety concerns and a poor therapeutic effect. Large MXene NSs are also unsuitable for intravenous (IV) delivery, which necessitates nanoparticles (NPs) for simple transport via our circulatory system as well as effective penetration and accumulation in cancer tissues [102]. As a result, the most effective use of etchant, etching duration, and intercalant kind are critical criteria to assure effective production of biocompatible MXene NSs, according to the overall research published on top-down production of MXene.

Bottom-up production strategy, which uses atomic scale control is a less well-known MXene synthesis process [84]. Small organic/inorganic molecules/atoms are typically used as the starting point for the bottom-up synthesis, which is then subsequently crystal development that may be arranged to produce the 2D-ordered layer [74]. The CVD technique is the most prevalent method for this approach, and it can manufacture high-quality thin films on a variety of substrates. The CVD creates ultrathin films, which are frequently ML (about 6-layer) in general [103]. When temperatures exceeding 1085 °C, first MXene formed by the CVD generated good quality very thin Mo2C utilizing CH4 gas as a carbon source and Cu/Mo foil substrate. The optimum growing temperature and duration resulted in a spectrum of films with lateral diameters ranging from 10 to 100 m. The Mo2C films produced were defect-free and crystallinity was high, indicating the absence of surface functional groups [104]. Unfortunately, it is not suited for biomedical uses since well functionalized MXenes NMs are an ideal for NSs surface engineering, and size (m) is also large for cell penetration.

Other approaches for MXene synthesis have been investigated, including the template method and plasma-enhanced pulsed laser deposition (PELPD) [105]. The first ultrathin Mo2C films produced by the PELPD utilizing CH4 plasma as a carbon source interacted with Mo vapor created by PLD method. The above process was carried out on a sapphire substrate that had been heat treated at 700 °C, in order to create high-quality films with controllable thickness by adjusting the laser pulse rate. Other changes have been reported, including the scalable salt-template production of the 2D nitrides like W2N, MoN, and V2N, by decreasing their relevant 2D hexagonal oxides with NH3 102. They discovered that the salt-templating may yield 2D metal oxide precursors, and that MoN-NSs formed from this production were hydrophilic and had a thickness of less than a nanometer when dispersed in water [105]. Other metal oxides, for instance, V2N and W2N NSs, have been successfully tested using this approach.

Nonetheless, there is a lack of knowledge on the bottom-up production approach of the biocompatible MXene, leaving opportunity for the development [90]. Another issue of both top-down and bottom-up production approach that needs to be researched further is the resilience and safety of large-scale synthesis processes. Table 2 summarizes the primary differences between MXene's top-down and bottom-up synthesis techniques, as well as material attributes highlighted. After synthesis, MXene surfaces can be further changed (independent of bottom-up/top-down) to improve biocompatibility and reduce cytotoxicity for biomedical applications.

MXene's electrical structure and hence its properties are altered when nitrogen is substituted for carbon. The bulk of MXenes found are carbides, with the exception of nitrides and carbonitrides. Attempts to produce MXenes with X = B (MBenes) had a limited success so far. Other computationally predicted 2D carbides, nitrides, and borides are still waiting to be synthesized. It should be attempted to synthesize them.

Etchant stability is one of the great challenges in making some of the theoretically predicted MXenes. As a result, new synthesis processes must be developed in order to achieve new MXene compositions and produce controlled and homogeneous surface terminations. The synthesis of Cr2CTx and Ti2NTx by etching Al from Cr2AlC and Ti2AlN respectively is an example of this. Earlier attempts to create MXenes by etching Al from these MAX phases with HF failed, resulting in the creation of fluorides and oxides. These MXenes could only be synthesized experimentally when different etchants were utilized; nevertheless, the synthesis needs to be replicated by other researchers to prove the validity of the technique. Another crucial aspect impacting the effectiveness of MXene's synthesis is the form of bonding between layers in parent bulk material. For example, to synthesize Mo2CTx, Mo2Ga2C is used instead of Mo2GaC because the Ga–Ga bonds are weaker that the Mo–Ga bonds, therefore the selective etching of Ga can start by breaking the Ga–Ga bonds [69].

Generally, regarding to MXenes NMs future aspect, modeling and simulation at various length and time scales have been used extensively to future predict the structure stability, properties, and performance of MXenes in different applications, and machine learning is gaining some momentum. However, many physical features of MXenes, like their electrical conductivity, still have theoretical limitations. Many additional expected features, including as ferromagnetism and topological insulator behavior will be tested in the lab. By overcoming these obstacles and significantly enhancing MXene synthesis and property characterization, new avenues to numerous essential applications can be opened.

Because of its novel convertible 2D in-layer nanosized structures as well as changeable chemical compositions, MXenes offer a wide range of biological uses. MXenes are an emerging rival for an effective cancer therapy and diagnostics among a range of the 2D nanostructures (e.g. MOS2, graphene, etc. [109,110]). Unique architectural and physicochemical properties are regarded as a pioneer tool in oncology, e.g.
1)
Cancer treatments: Photo-thermal therapy (PTT), photodynamic therapy (PDT), radiotherapy and chemo.
2)
Cancer diagnosis: Sensing electrode NMs, different means in magnetic resonance imaging (MRI)/computerized tomography (CT) Scan bioimaging, and photo-acoustic (PA) imaging.
3)
Cancer theranostics: Cargo for encapsulating anticancer drug and contrast agent for both.

MXenes have versatile properties in the biomedical applications due to their distinctive transformable 2D in-layer nanosized structures and somehow convenient chemical nano-compositions. Fascinating physicochemical properties of the MXenes show promise for a range of biological applications. These 2D multi-functional MXenes and their NCs are now being formed for PTT, photodynamic/photothermal/chemosynergistic treatment, diagnostic imaging, anti-microbial, and biosensing applications. MXenes, in addition to the PDT/PTT agents are also frequently used as a wonderful cargo for delivering cancer treatments. MXenes' medicinal properties include the development of the reactive oxygen species (ROS) for PDT based death of cancer cells, heat formation via photo-thermal technique to accelerate tumor cell ablation, and cargo for anti-cancer medication liberation to the specific locations [111]. The majority of MXene compositions, for example Ti3C2Tx, Nb2CTx, and Ta4C3Tx, are biocompatible, low cytotoxic, their plasmon resonance and elevated PTT based efficiency in Infrared (IR)/near-IR (NIR) range known them suitable for cancer theranostics (Fig. 3 (a)). Ta4C3Tx has gotten a lot of interest since it has greater photothermal efficiency (44.7%) than Ti3C2Tx. In-vitro and in-vivo photothermal ablation of breast cancer cells using soybean phospholipid (SP) modified Ta4C3Tx NSs (137), >90% of MXene-incubated cells were destroyed when an NIR laser was applied (Fig. 3(b)).

MXenes have also been employed for medication administration and tumor heating guided by MRI. MXenes are more resistant to bio-fouling and the growth of germs and bacteria than graphene oxide, making them ideal for filtration and desalination membranes as well as implantable devices. In comparison to gold microelectrodes, Ti3C2Tx were employed in implantable brain electrodes with improved impedance and in-vivo neural recording capability (Fig. 3(c)). MXene electrode arrays have demonstrated similar performance in high-density, high-resolution surface electromyography, outperforming noble metals and allowing gel-free application. A Ti3C2Tx Trichloroethylene (TCE) on acrylate was investigated in intraocular lenses to emulate accommodating function of eye's natural lens, inspired by its intriguing electro-optical features. Fig. 3(d) shows model of adjustable-focus lens made up of spin-coated Ti3C2Tx electrodes and a liquid crystal that can vary its refractive index (under an electric field) and hence modify its optical power and focus. Adsorption of urea from dialysate by Ti3C2Tx and Mo2TiC2Tx MXenes is more efficient than other sorbents and might lead to the creation of a wearable/flexible electronic device known as an artificial kidney. The small slits in functionalized and charged MXene NSs that act as adsorption sites, contribute to the high adsorption effectiveness for the urea and uric pollutants [65]. Further details treatment results and recovery are shown in Fig. 3(e–i). The next part will go through extensive research on the MXenes' current use in oncological applications, as well as associated issues. So far, these 2D multifunctional MXenes NMs and their NCs were used for the PTT, PDT, chemo synergistic-therapy, diagnostic-imaging, anti-microbial, and bio-sensing applications. Various therapeutic applications of the MXenes NMs are discussed in this section. Some of the specialized applications in the biomedical industry that have been classified thus far include biosensors, antibacterial activity, bioimaging, treatments, and theranostics etc.

The 2D NSs various physicochemical properties open up a wide range of diagnostic imaging possibilities, means that they might be utilized as the CAs to increase imaging for diagnosis performance. MXene-based agents are distinguished from conventional one by quantum size effects in the PL cellular imaging, intrinsic photothermal results in PA imaging, element-boosted contrast in X-ray CT imaging, and successful shipment of functional CAs in MRI.

Fluorescence microscopy is extensively used bioimaging methods in bioresearch as a result of its simplicity and cheap apparatus. Although its modest penetration depth (e.g. about 1500 m in a dense gray matter medium under 3-photon stimulation), this approach is particularly convenient for the bioimaging [115]. Inorganic 2D NMs and their associated quantum dots (QDs) have exhibited amazing fluorescent features for bioimaging, like changeable wavelength, high photo-stability, and optimum quantum yields, when compared to ordinary organic fluoresce. Graphene QDs, for example, being the first known 2D metal-free fluorescent NMs, have shown promising applications in efficient fluorescence imaging. The ultrathin liquid-exfoliated g-C3N4 and associated g-C3N4 QDs have a high photoluminescence (PL) quantum yield for intracellular bioimaging [116,117].

MXenes can be utilized as image contrast agents in bioimaging, in addition to their biosensing and antimicrobial properties. In general, ‘traditionally’ synthesized MXenes have modest luminosity in aqueous solutions [118], although their fluorescent characteristics can be improved. One of the most promising approaches is to bind a fluorescent species on the MXenes surface. The Ti3C2 loaded with fluorescent medicine doxorubicin (DOX) and hyaluronic acid (HA) for tumor marking was disclosed by Liu et al., [119]. Electrostatic adsorption of cationic DOX and Al oxyanion-terminated MXene in the LBL resulted in formation of conjugates. The approach might be employed in anticancer treatment as well as bioimaging. According to Xue et al. [120], Ti3C2 based QDs were generated by cutting the NMs. The reaction temperature had an effect on the diameter of the resulting MXenes based QD (2.9–6.2 nm for T = 100–150 °C; average MXenes based QD thickness was 1 nm). The MXenes based QDs' photo-luminescence spectrum, like those of other carbon-based fluorescent NMs is excitation-dependent (e.g. graphene QDs [120]). When absorption switches from UV (340 nm) to a visible (440 nm), the PL production with the highest shifts from blue to red, and two types of such MXenes based QDs were investigated for in-vitro bioimaging [121]. Wang et al. [122], used an aqueous tetramethylammoniumhydroxide delamination method followed by the sonication to create fluorescent Ti3C2 NFs (0.5–1.5 nm thick with a lateral dimension of 9 nm) for MXene based bioimaging. The Ti3C2 samples showed excitation reliant emission, with excitation shift from 340 to 500 nm and related emission peak shift from 460 to 580 nm [122]. The Ti3C2 QDs are luminous. Under various synthetic conditions, the physiochemical characteristics and the PL principle of as-prepared Ti3C2 QDs (Fig. 4 (a)) and their TEM characterization (Fig. 4(b-g)) have been carefully examined [123]. These Ti3C2 QDs generated at about 100 °C (QDs-100) exhibit excitation reliant PL performance with a high quantum yield of up to 9.9-percent that may be related to the strong quantum confinement effect (QCE) (Fig. 4(h-j)). As demonstrated in combined CLSM picture, MXenes based QDs were simply taken up by endocytosis route. The use of the MXenes QDs to label RAW264.7 cells as well as multicolor cellular imaging reagents, indicating the tremendous potential of the MXene based QDs for uses in the optics, biomedicine, and cellular imaging. Geng et al. [122], for example, reported a method for manufacturing ultra-small MXenes under mild circumstances utilizing intralayer cutting and delamination approach in aqueous tetramethylammonium hydroxide (TMAOH). So far, ultra-small Ti3C2 MXenes show an excitation-dependent emission and high optical absorption. Significantly, a process like this might be utilized to create ultra-small QDss of other MXene family members like as Ti2C and Nb2C. Wang et al., [124] investigated the luminous characteristics of solvo-thermal treated Ti3C2Tx in DMF and proved their use in cellular imaging.

Due to 3D tomography of an atomic configuration founded on various X-ray absorptions in lesions and tissues, X-ray CT has become a popular medical imaging method. Fluorescence-based methods however, are inappropriate due to low penetration of UV/visible light in tissues; it can't be used to make medical diagnoses or in-vivo study. As a result, alternate technologies for seeing living organisms, such as X-ray radiation or ultra-sonography [126] have been developed. The X-ray CT is a strong diagnostic technique due to its elevated resolution, non-invasiveness, and capability to construct 3D images [127]. Iodine based compounds are the most often employed as the CT contrast agents, despite the fact that they have limited blood circulation durations are poisonous, and can cause kidney damage [128]. A better way for the CT scan based imaging is to employ materials with additional biocompatible components.

The CT-CAs has been studied extensively in NMs with high atomic number (Z) elements. WS2-PEG as a CA for the CT bioimaging has been successfully created because of greater Z of W (Z = 74) than clinical application of I element (Z = 53) [129]. Bismuth (Z = 83) was predicted to be a viable candidate in the CT CA due to its significantly higher Z than other metal elements and better biocompatibility. As a result, 2D topological Bi2Se3 NSs reveal strong X-ray reduction and can improve in-vivo CT imaging of tumor tissue [130]. The Ta4C3 MXenes based materials are appealing applicants for CT imaging contrast agents because of their ideal NPs size, outstanding biocompatibility, and environmental friendly manufacture [131]. The Ta4C3 MXenes, a Ta-based compound with a high Z of 73, may surely function as a CT CA in this regard. In addition to enhanced Ta4C3-SP concentrations, the Hounsfield unit (HU) value and related the CT images of Ta4C3-SP dispersed in the Xanthan gum revealed significant signal improvement. The scheme and Ta4C3-SP's in vitro CT imaging outperforms commercial iodine-based CT CAs in terms of contrast (Fig. 5 (a-c)). Furthermore, the tumor-bearing mice were administered Ta4C3-SP intravenously for the in-vivo testing. The CT images obtained 24-h after injection reveal considerable tumor contrast improvement varying from 83.8 to 232.32-percent HU (Fig. 5(d and e)). As a consequence, these Ta4C3 MXenes NMs might be useful CAs for the CT imaging.

MRI is widely intended for clinically diagnostic imaging as a result of its high spatial resolution, good contrasting difference of the 3D soft tissues, and non-invasive nature [132]. Integration of functional components with the 2D NSs opens up new possibilities for multi-functionalization of 2D nano-systems, greatly expanding their diagnostic and imaging applications. For example, the combination of MnOx and Ti3C2 benefits Mn-based MRI that were studied for diagnostic tumor MR imaging. The Mn-based MRI is mostly utilized imaging modalities in clinics as a result of its enhanced spatial resolution and favorable tissue contrast. When the MnOx component of MnOx/Ti3C2 arrives at the acidic tumor microenvironment (TME), it has unique pH-responsive T1-weighted MRI capacity. The T1-weighted MRI indicated a distinct concentration-dependent increase, as well as an acidic-induced positive MRI signal amplification [133]. In-vivo T1-weighted MRI of the MnOx/Ti3C2-SP NSs was performed on the tumor-bearing animals. The successful accumulation of MnOx/Ti3C2-SP NSs via the enhanced permeability and retention effect (EPR) effect and Mn-ion let loose in mild acidic TME resulted in a significant enhancement of MRI contrasts in tumor areas. In another case, we developed MnOx/Ta4C3 NC NSs that functioned as high performance contrast agents for the TME-responsive T1-weighted MR imaging, with the MnOx component also being employed [133].

Dai et al. [133], studied different ways to create a MnOx/Ti3C2 NCs for imaging using PA and MRI. The MRI is an additional excellent non-invasive technology for imaging tissues in patients, with properties comparable to the CT but devoid of applying dangerous X-rays. It can be used alternatively by patients who are allergic to the CT contrast agents [134]. The most commonly used MRI contrast agents include gadolinium, which has been associated with an increased risk of renal damage [135]. As a result, the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) has approved MnCl2.4H2O as a safe and effective MRI contrast agent [136]. As a result, biocompatible Mn-containing MRI contrast agents with therapeutic promise have emerged. As nanosized Mn2O3 is generated straightforwardly on Ti3C2 surface, like NCs were employed in tumor micro-environments as responsive MRI contrast agents [137]. Liu et al. [138], developed Ta4C3/super-paramagnetic iron oxide (IONP) NCs to generate agents for single or multifunctional imaging methods. The CT imaging was achievable due to the existence of tantalum, and IONP portion permitted the material to be applied in MRI. Using a numerous technique, Zong et al. [139], recorded the deposited GdW10 on Ti3C2 NSs. Resulting NCs might be applied for MRI as well as CT imaging since the functionalized MXene includes tungsten and gadolinium. MXene QDs like graphene and carbon-QDs emitted light, and their emissions were excitation-dependent, as previously indicated. MXene nano-flacks (NFs) may be broken down into extremely small QDs by hydro-thermal treatments, resulting in quantum confinement with a size effect and defect-induced luminescence. These MXene QDs were suitable for cell imaging after adequate surface modifications. Such as, Zhi et al. [120], discovered that temperature of hydro-thermal method influenced the MXene luminescence significantly improve (Fig. 6 (a)). The luminescence of MXenes increased when the treatment temperature was elevated from 100 to 150 °C. The narrow bandgap (B.G) (∼0.1eV) of Ti3C2 MXenes might be widened by quantum processes, allowing for light emission. The luminescence might be induced by high quantum confinement caused by the tiny lateral size (∼10 nm; Fig. 6(b and c)). The luminescence of low-temperature annealed MXene QDs was assumed to be mostly owing to size constraint of MXene NMs, with a little part from surface defects. Number of surface defects increased as the temperature increased that enhanced the brightness of the MXene QDs. Also, L. Zhou et al. [124], discovered analogous luminescence from hydrothermally formed Ti3C2 in DMF and employed these NMs for cell imaging. The fluorescence, on the other hand, was linked to the treatment's creation of graphene QDs. Geng et al. [122], recently discovered a technique for manufacturing the MXene QDs in a moderate environment without the need for hydrothermal treatment using TMAOH (Fig. 6(d)). The photo-stability and excitation-dependent emissions of the as-prepared ultra-small MXene QDs (Fig. 6(f, e)) were good (Fig. 6(g)) [122]. In addition to PA IMAGES and luminescence imaging, MXenes were applied as X-ray/CT contrast agents. The Ta4C3 is presently studied as X-ray/CT contrast agents because of the high Z (Z = 73) of the Ta-element [125]. Furthermore, MXene conductivity may be exploited for bioimaging by detecting changes in electrical signal in specific conditions. B. Xu et al. [140], for example, utilized MXenes on a pattern scaffold for neuron cell culture. Changes in the field-effect of MXenes can be utilized to evaluate cell activity since their conductivity is sensitive to their surroundings. This technology can monitor brain activity.

In general, the CAs for Photo-Acoustic (PA) imaging might be efficient photothermal conversion agents. The PA imaging, a developing technology that uses the PA effect to defeat the high degree of optical photon scattering in living tissue, provides living individuals with greater spatial resolution and permits imaging of deeper tissues than other optical imaging methods. Light absorption by molecules causes a thermally induced pressure jump, which causes ultrasonic waves to be launched those are detected by acoustic detectors and used to produce pictures [141,142]. Because many illnesses, especially in their early stages, do not have a natural PA contrast, it is important to use a PA contrast agent. Many traditional 2D nano-systems have been designed that provide significant contrasts under PA imaging, including graphene, MoOx, and WS2 [143,144]. Another strategy that employs the PA imaging using MXenes to visualize tissue is based on transforming optical light into acoustic radiation. The PA on the other hand, has a limited penetration depth that is dependent on the laser wavelength. Furthermore, it uses non-ionizing laser energy in the visible or infrared ranges rather than harmful X-rays [126]. Lin et al., demonstrated utilization of Nb2C-polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) as PA contrast agent [145]. The Ta4C3 has also been demonstrated to be appropriate for the CT and PA bioimaging of cancers. Lin et al., studied using Ta4C3 NSs stabilized on the surface with SP. Colloidal stability is a vital feature that affects the performance of nanostructures in the living tissues [146]. It increases biocompatibility of NMs. MXenes NMs; particularly Ta4C3 MXenes were estimated to play a role in this. Because of strong photothermal conversion efficiency of Ta4C3 MXene in NIR bio-window, PA imaging was investigated using Ta4C3-SP NSs as CAs [125]. The contrast-enhancement functions of Ta4C3-SP at various concentrations are clearly illustrated by in-vitro PA images, as are the corresponding Ta4C3-SP concentrations (Fig. 7 (a-c)). The mice with tumors were then given an intravenous infusion of Ta4C3-SP, and in-vivo PA images have been obtained at various time intervals (Fig. 7(d and e)).

In contrast to pre-treatment image, PA signal of tumor area shows a time-dependent lightning development with intensity increasing from 0.39 to 1.0a.u., a highly large value in ∼24 hs post injection, possibly due to passive Ta4C3 MXene NSs accumulation via EPR effect [147]. Many groups have previously reported on different MXene and their compounds for in-vitro and in-vivo PA imaging, with Ti3C2, Nb2C, Ta4C3, and MnOx/Ti3C2, all using NIR light [133]. MXenes has well-developed imaging techniques including as PA images and luminescence imaging. Because of its low tissue-attenuation coefficient, PA images, emerging diagnostic imaging modality can overcome optical imaging infiltration limit by identifying pressure waves that were generated with laser-irradiated tissues, allowing for real-time monitoring of the biological configuration and function. An effective PA images contrast agent must have a high photothermal conversion capacity in order to have a significantly higher PA signal than nearby tissue. MXenes have high photothermal conversion efficiency, building them an excellent for the PA images. The Localized Surface Plasmon Resonance (LSPR) effect of semimetal MXene NSs is responsible for the tremendous broad spectrum light absorption and conversion efficiency in MXenes [112]. Traditional MAX has been found to exhibit metal-like properties, but exfoliated MXene NSs have an energy band structure resembling that of a semimetal, akin to TiS2 NSs, and can quickly induce LSPR phenomena, exactly like metal NPs. A range of MXenes have been proven absorption of excitation light, conversion of the photon energy into crystal vibrations, and discharge of the energy as a heat macroscopically. In a range of MXene compositions, the photothermal effect has been proven (e.g. Ti3C2, Nb2C, Ta4C3) [133]. MXenes also have broad and powerful absorption spectra that extend into the NIR-region, making them an ideal for deep-tissue PA images. Under 704 nm light, Chen et al. [133], studied Ti3C2, Nb2C, and many other MXene compositions for in-vivo PA images and Ta4C3 (Fig. 8 (a-i)). Similarly, the Liu et al., created Ti3C2 for PA images using a fluorine-free method [148]. MXene-based PA images allow theranostics by providing real-time PTT monitoring. MXenes were employed for the luminescent cell imaging using MXene QDs that were newly developed.

Sensors are used to monitor the concentrations of many substances to guarantee human and ecological protection, such as pesticides in the food [41]. Low limit of detection (LOD), high sensitivity, quick reaction time, broad linear range, and strong selectivity for a single analyte or set of analytes are the main sensor performance requirements [149]. Aside from therapeutic and diagnostic imaging applications, 2D NMs might potentially be used as innovative biosensing methods for the recognition of bio-macromolecules and bio-effects. They ought to be economical to facilitate industrialization. The biocompatible (bio)sensors for analyte detection have been described for 2D NMs such as graphene [150] and MoS2 [151], and a new group of 2D family called MXene NMs [10] are presently investigated for this reason. MXenes were widely used to detect a range of gases by varying their surface terminations, which have a significant influence on their properties. MXenes were used as nanoplatforms for protein, enzyme, and nucleic acid loading due to their high biocompatibility and abundance of anchoring sites. MXene sensing layer deposition substantially simplifies anchoring of bio-identified components onto electrode surfaces that is critical for enhancing sensitivity and selectivity of surface-based biosensors. The superior conductivity of MXenes also aids in conversion of bio-signals to quantifiable electrical/optical responses, shining light on flexible biosensors for target concentration measurement.

Currently, the most critical features of the process of charge transfer during gas molecule addition and the consequent conductivity are the basis for MXene-based gas sensors. Yu et al. [152], for example, forecasted the potential of the MXenes in gas sensors by using Density-Functional Theory (DFT) modeling. Based on the DFT results, they determined that SL Ti2C with O2 terminations was highly sensitive to NH3 vs. other gas molecules for instance, nitrogen, H2, NO2, CH4, CO2, CO and O2, as shown in Fig. 9 (a). The interaction of NH3 with O2-terminated semiconductor MXenes e.g. M2C (M = Ti, Sc, Hf, and Zr) with diverse charge states was studied by the DFT simulation. Their findings demonstrated that the NH3 molecules were well adsorbed on O2-terminated M2C with obvious charge transfer, and that NH3 release were performed easily by adjusting the electrons pumped into M2C [153]. Furthermore, Kim et al. [154], proved Ti3C2's ability to detect NH3. Aside from NH3, the MXene-based H2O2 sensor was theoretically and practically investigated [155]. The H2O2 has potential to successfully oxidize MXene surface terminations, resulting in a significant rise in O2 density on their surface and, as a result, enhancing charge transfer. For example, Wang et al. [156], studied TiO2-NPs/Ti3C2 NCs. They employed this approach to immobilize hemoglobin (Hb) to develop biosensor free of mediators (Fig. 9(b)). According to spectroscopic and electrochemical studies, the TiO2–Ti3C2 NC is a bio-compatible and effective matrix for redox protein immobilization, ensuring protein bioactivity and stability. Unusual organ-like hybrid configuration of the TiO2–Ti3C2 facilitates direct electron transfer of Hb, allowing for a broad range of 0.1–380 mM for H2O2 detection with extraordinarily low LOD of 14 nM (Fig. 9(c)). Besides H2O2, scientists determined that system had a high sensitivity for detecting nitrite. Also the NH3 and H2O2, MXenes were formed to detect other small molecules like, glucose and phenol [155]. R.B.Rakhi et al. [155], developed glucose oxidase (GOx) immobilized Au/MXene NC for sensitive glucose detection.

Biosensor electrode displayed good electrocatalytic activity, with low LOD of 5.9 mM and a broad linear range of glucose detection from 0.1 to 18 mM. Aside from tiny molecules, interactions between MXenes and metal ions may result in a doping cause, changing MXene properties. For example, X. Zhu et al. [157], studied electro-chemical response of MXenes to detect co-existing Pb2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, and Cu2+ discovered a sensitivity that was higher than previous findings. They determined that each heavy metal peak potential was clearly described and appropriately divided in simultaneous voltammetric measurement of aforementioned four target heavy metal ions (HMIs). Furthermore, the detection limits for these HMIs were 0.1 mM for Cu2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, and 1 mM for Hg2+ (Fig. 9(d)). Numerous MXenes (Ti3C2 and M2X; M = Ti, Hf, Nb, V, Mo, Ta, Cr, and X = CorN) have been studied to capable of removing HMIs as well as detecting them sensitively [159]. MXenes have also shown promise as in-vivo pH sensors. X. Chen et al. [160], shown that luminescence of Ti3C2 MXene QDs is pH dependent. As the pH rises from 5 to 9, the strength of their absorption decreases by around 10%. This behavior was attributed to the protonation or de-protonation of the Ti3C2 MXene QDs. Due to de-protonation on MXene QD surface, a fraction of valence electrons were trapped and not liberated to contribute in photo-absorption, resulting in decreased absorption. Furthermore, when the pH rose, a part of luminous surface defects became non-luminescent, resulting in increased non-radiative energy losses and a decrease in Ti3C2 MXene QDs emission. Adding [Ru(dpp)3] to the pH-independent [Ru(dpp)3], this new ratiometric fluorescent pH sensor confirmed sensitive pH detection from 5 to 9 using Cl2 as the reference dye on Ti3C2 QDs. Recent study has revealed that the MXenes have the potential for Raman sensing, opening the door to biosensing. For example, Yoshimura and coworkers studied the synthesis of Au-, Pd- and Ag-@MXene hybrids as SERS substrates. For sensitive methylene blue (MB) SERS detection, metal-loaded MXenes were utilized as SERS substrate, yielding an amplification factor of the order of 105. The A. Sarycheva et al. [158], recently examined the use of pristine Ti3C2 and Ti2N as SERS substrates for R6G Raman tag detection, with enhancement factors of 106 and 1012, correspondingly (Fig. 9(e)). Table 3 shows Sensors based on MXene and their properties results.

The biosensing nano-platforms are separated into two groups based on the type of output signals, and then further subdivided based on distinct sensing methodologies, with the recent up to date advancements in MXene biosensors discussed. In next sections we will describe in detail the most important physicochemical features and compatibility of MXenes for use in various biosensing technologies, whereas will summarize details concerning targets such as enzymes, nucleic acids, proteins, and LOD of the MXene-based sensors. Although Ti3C2Tx is used in the majority of contemporary MXene NMs based biosensors. The word MXene really refers to a variety of 2D NMs with biosensing potential.

The Ti3C2Tx MXene's metallic conductive nature makes it ideal for building electrical nanoplatforms for quick and sensitive biomarker identification. This section covers electrochemical methods and devices used in the MXene-based biosensing applications, ranging from traditional enzymatic electrodes to electrochemical micro-fluidics and sensitive bio-FETs, and photo-electrochemical detections. In next section we will present further information regarding MXene-based electrochemical sensors.

Electrochemical analysis remains the most extensively used detection technology in the biosensing field because of its benefits in competent transducing, high sensitivity, low LOD, speedy analysis, easy operation, and low cost [206]. Amperometry and chronoamperometry are two electrochemical methods extensively employed in enzymatic biosensors. The basic arrangement of constant potential facilitates quick electrochemical analysis due to the specific redox reactions of enzymes. Amperometric approaches might not be capable to offer sufficient information for bio-detections when complicated redox processes are involved, hence Cyclic Voltammetry (CV) is recommended to extend the scanning potential window. The CV approach used numerous redox peaks to differentiate various targets. On this premise, the square wave voltammetry (SWV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) approaches eliminate more background current signals, allowing for a higher LOD. As a result, immune-sensors and nucleic acid sensors frequently use the SWV and DPV. The application of electrochemical approaches, on the other hand is not limited by biosensor categories. Enzymatic electrodes combine enzymes with biocompatible electrode surfaces for selective catalysis of redox processes and direct electron transfer [207], making them one of the most typical electrochemical implementations. Due to the limited surface areas and sluggish charge kinetics of traditional electrode materials, a large over-potential is required to accelerate electrochemical processes [208]. On the other hand, using a high potential window makes it difficult to maintain enzyme bioactivities and compromises biosensing stability. Ti3C2 MXene was initially described as a matrix for hemoglobin (Hb) immobilization and the development of nitrite biosensors to increase electrochemical signals and enzyme stability [184]. The biosensor has a large linear range and an exceedingly low LOD of 0.12 M when Ti3C2 was used as the Hb carrier. The increased biosensing performance was attributable to Ti3C2 MXene's large surface area and microenvironment, which aided substrate trapping and enzyme access.

In-situ development of the TiO2-NPs on Ti3C2 enabled Hb immobilization and the direct electron transfer method, owing to the deep burying of the electroactive core in protein [156]. Enzyme entrapment was improved by the organ-like Ti3C2/TiO2 hybrid, and the limited area increased the effective collision between enzymes and substrate, boosting sensing performance. Also, for malathion detection, Ag-NPs modified Ti3C2 was used as a nano-platform to enhance an electron transport between acetylcholinesterase (AChE) and electrode [172]. Despite the direct enzyme immobilization on organ-like MXene [183], exfoliated the Ti3C2 MXene was coated on working electrode as matrix for glucose oxidase (GOx) immobilization by TBA intercalation. The TBA intercalated Ti3C2 glucose biosensor has a greater heterogeneous electron transfer rate with a wider exposure range than pure Ti3C2 MXene (Fig. 10 (a and b)). The development of SL Ti3C2, which reduced the charge transfer distance between enzyme and electrodes, was credited with better glucose sensing. For continuous multi-component monitoring in blood, an electrochemical micro-fluidic system founded on the screen-printed Electrodes with MXene recently presented [209]. The micro-fluidic chip has two functioning electrodes, one for creatinine detection and the other for uric acid (UA) and urea detection simultaneously (Fig. 10(c)). The MB was used as a reference molecule in an electrochemical device to modify the background signal for accurate UA calculations in this study. Urea was coupled to electrode interfaces by creating H2 bonds with the MXene surfaces terminations (OH or F). The hydroxyl ions produced by urea catalysis affect the pH of the solution as a result, redox potential of the UA. Calibration equations were constructed by examining the UA's oxidation peak current and peak potential drift. Because EUA is unaffected by UA concentration and has greater linearity, the concentration of urea evaluated using the Curea-EUA calibration curve. Three electrodes (source, drain, and gate) plus a semiconductor channel that connects them make up a FET device. Current flowing via drain-source electrodes is electro-statically regulated by introducing an electric field perpendicular to the dielectric layer, which is known as the gating effect. The gating effect in bio-FETs, on the other hand is achieved by bio-functionalizing dielectric layers that is selectively trap bio-molecules or analytes and convert their binding behaviors into electrical signals [210]. The Ti3C2Tx MXene has recently been used as the channel material in FET devices [211]. FETs made from SL and bulk Ti3C2Tx had good conductivity (2.310.57 m and 15.81.3 m, respectively), indicating that low-resistance electric contacts were created between Ti3C2Tx NSs.

Ultrathin Ti3C2 films might be micro-patterned for bio-FET production due to their high conductivity and mechanical strength [140]. As shown in Fig. 10(d), the Ti3C2 FET was used to conduct sensitive detection of neurotransmitter and neuro-electric signal. Variation in the conductivity of the FET might indicate binding events in real-time when dopamine, a common neurotransmitter was free to interact with Ti3C2 surfaces. MXene also showed remarkable biocompatibility for in-vitro neuron cultivation and spiking activity monitoring (Fig. 10(e)). With a high correlation value of 0.82, the current measurements were very similar to the fluorescence variations, exhibiting excellent temporal resolution (Fig. 10(f)). The combination of appropriate B.G and excellent conductivity of OH and/or F terminated MXene was credited with the high detection sensitivity. MXene-based bio-FETs for H1N1 (influenza virus A) and SARS-CoV-2 (severe acute respiratory syndrome corona-virus 2) sensing are one of the most recent discoveries [212].

The Ti3C2 MXene-graphene NCs based FET channels used MXene's abundant binding sites to immobilize antibodies for selective antigen identification, and graphene to allow charge transmission. When the H1N1 virus and SARS-CoV-2 spike protein were loaded into the bio-FETs, they caused significant variations in source-drain current and gate voltage (Fig. 10(g and h)), allowing for sensitive point-of-care testing to detect ecological viruses with low viral loads. While the MXene-based bio-FETs are still in their infancy, MXene family's emergence compensates for the shortcomings of prior graphene (lack of B.G) or TMDs (poor conductivity) based FETs. The FET biosensors, in general, necessitate suitable semiconductor channels with electrical transportation properties that are sensitive to the surface charge distribution [214]. The MXene offers a highly engineerable band configuration as well as good conductivity, allowing for the fabrication of functionalized FETs [215]. For example, Ti2C(OH)xFy MXene may be used to make both n- and p-channel FET devices [216], while Ta2C/MoS2 interfaces with differing terminations could be used to convert between Ohmic and Schottky contacts [217]. MXene's great tunability should allow for a lot of customization and flexibility in MXene-based systems.

As a result of excitation light sources and transducing electrodes are separated, the photo-electrochemical (PEC) sensors have lower background noise and higher sensitivity detection than the standard electrochemical assays. Photoactive materials, such as semiconductors, play an important role in PEC sensing because they form electron-hole pairs when photons are absorbed. MXene has been used as photo-generated carrier separators in the PEC sensing because it is a promising 2D optoelectrical material. Apart from easy contact with analytes owing to the hydrophilic surface, MXenes provide a high SSA for intimate semiconductor assembly or development, which speeds up electron migration. Furthermore, highly conductive Ti3C2 improves photo-generated carrier mobility, which improves the photoelectric conversion efficiency. Because, Ti3C2 can straightforwardly form Schottky junctions with photoactive semiconductors, a Ti3C2/Cu2O heterostructure was used as a working electrode with Ag/AgCl as a reference for glucose detection with great sensitivity [213].

The Ti3C2 MXene's low work function makes it a suitable hole mediator, allowing holes to flow from p-type Cu2O to Ti3C2 and external circuits (Fig. 10(i)). The PEC glucose biosensors were sensitive to dissolve O2 that was thought to be an electron acceptor in the reduction reaction. The photocurrent of Ti3C2/Cu2O reduced noticeably when the superoxide radical scavenged in DMSO, but increasing H2O2 led to larger photocurrent. The addition of glucose, on this basis, used dissolved O2 that partly decreased products, blocking transfer channel of photo-generated electrons and causing ‘signal off’ cathode photocurrent. Apart from Schottky junction-based PEC sensors, a Z-scheme structure founded on the TiO2/Ti3C2Tx/Cu2O [169] has been presented, with Ti3C2Tx MXene serving as electron mediator in dual-photoexcitation systems. Heterostructure thought to boost glucose detection sensitivity and photocurrent conversion efficiency. On the other hand, ‘signal on’ PEC biosensing produced by adjusting adsorption of the photoactive surface on electrode. The Bi2S3/Ti3C2 Schottky junction was used to create a label-free PEC test for methyltransferase (MTase) [218]. For a large photocurrent response Bi2S3/Ti3C2 heterostructure was created, in which Ti3C2 MXene not only controlled photogenerated carrier recombination, but also selectively absorbed DNA via H2 bonds and chelation interactions in Ti-ions and phosphate groups, as shown in Fig. 10(j).

In the absence of MTase, Hpall could cleave the DNA probe, preventing Bi2S3/Ti3C2 from connecting to the electrode, resulting in near-zero background signals. Because methylated DNA are not be cleaved by Hpall after the addition of MTase, Bi2S3/Ti3C2 was absorbed on the electrode and photocurrent was generated upon light stimulation. Meanwhile, the Ti3C2-QDs have a wide and hydrophilic surface area that allows them to engage strongly with target molecules and photoactive supports [219]. The Ti3C2-QDs are appealing photoactive materials in the PEC sensing because of their anisotropic carrier mobility and high redox property [170]. The charge transportation resistance of the Ti3C2 QDs modified TiO2 electrode was substantially lower, indicating significantly quicker photogenerated carrier migration. Furthermore, the BiVO4/Ti3C2Tx photoanode improved Hg2+ PEC detection by using the photo-generated hole capture ability of Ti3C2Tx MXene [168]. The consumption of holes in Ti3C2Tx was prevented by selective chelation between Hg2+ and glutathione, thus a'signal off’ photocurrent are used for sensitive and selective.

MXenes are suitable photon-electron mediators and light-to-heat transfer interfaces due to their high light adsorption capability. In this part we will explain the optical nanoplatforms based MXene that transform bio-bindings into optical signals, which are becoming increasingly popular. Fluorescent and electro-chemi-luminescence sensors, in general, have an excellent sensitivity and low LODs, making them an ideal for laboratory examination. Plasmonic sensors, for example surface plasmon resonance (SPR) and surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS), are potential biomarker detection approaches with the low LODs. Colorimetric sensors, on the other hand, are more like semi-quantitative or quantitative test strips with lower LODs than other optical sensors.

To establish a link between the ECL intensity and target focus, the ECL test uses light emission from redox reactions of electro-generated luminous species. Because of MXene's high conductivity and catalytic nature, it dramatically increased the redox efficiency of the luminophore and facilitated the electron transfer method in the ECL structure. Aptamer-modified Ti3C2 MXene nano-probes based on a standard sandwich structure have been used to selectively detect MCF-7 (Michigan Cancer Foundation-7) exosomes, the combination of which greatly accelerated the redox of luminol and light emission. The LOD was 125particles.L−1 due to good linearity between ECL intensity and exosome logarithm concentration (from 5 × 102 to 5 × 106particleL−1). MXene/metal-NPs based NCs were also proposed for a more improved ECL test, leveraging Ti3C2 MXene's high reduction capacity [220]. The electrocatalytic activity of the Au-NPs deposited in-situ on MXene-aptamer nanoprobes towards luminol was reported [221]. The Au-NPs-MXene-Apt combination showed outstanding exosome biosensing capability, with a LOD of 30particles.L−1. Furthermore, for label-free finding of the single-nucleotide mismatch, a solid-state ECL sensor based on direct absorption of Ru(bpy)32+ on the MXene decorated GCE was presented [222]. MXene provided an abundance of binding sites for luminophore and oligonucleotide in this example, considerably increasing ECL intensity (Fig. 11 (a and b)). The ECL intensity of a 100 nM DNA sequence with a single-nucleotide mismatch is around 3.5-times that of a perfectly complementary sequence at the same concentration, according to detection in human urine matrix. In addition to traditional ECL luminophore, the mixture of the MXene and g–C3N4–NPs not only accommodated high amounts of luminophore but also inhibited g-C3N4 passivation [223]. As shown in Fig. 11(c), phosphorylated kemptide could chelate to enriched Ti defects in MXene/g-C3N4 hybrids in the presence of kinase and adenosine triphosphate, and the concentration of phosphorylated kemptide was subsequently transduced to ECL signal generated by g-C3N4. It was also shown that extremely conductive MXene may channel over-transferred electrons to the g-C3N4, considerably extending the ECL system's stability. The activity of protein kinase A was investigated further in kinase inhibitors, anywhere ECL intensity was employed to identify kinase activity in simulated samples containing forskolin, ellagic acid, and PBS (phosphate buffer saline) (Fig. 11(d)). Extended ECL features were also realized when 2D MXene was synthesized into QDs, which might be attributed to the aligned configuration, plentiful edge sites, and QCE [224]. Ti3C2Tx were consistently tuned to the QDs with definite configuration and sizes of 3–4.8 nm using TMAOH (tetramethylammonium hydroxide) aided by thermal reflux. In the existence of coreactant tri-n-propylamine (TPrA), annihilation ECL performance of the Ti3C2Tx-QDs was greatly enhanced by 29-times, giving Ti3C2Tx-QDs possible uses in ECL sensing [224]. Ti3C2Tx-QDs were more investigated with optimal cathodic ECL at neutral pH using persulfate as the coreactant, which shows promise in ECL assays under physiological settings [225].

Fluorescent biosensors, unlike other sensing methods, are generally solution-based. The probes need strong dispersion in the solutions with no aggregation, with visible fluorescence within the visible band, for improved measurement of fluorescence intensity. MXene QDs and NSs satisfy the criteria thanks to their outstanding hydrophilicity and adjustable electronic architectures. The nanoscale and structure of the MXene are altered, giving rise to a new material family with novel optical characteristics. Take Ti3C2Tx MXene for example: Ti3C2Tx-QDs with sizes of numerous NMs showed light emission capability under photo excitation, whereas Ti3C2Tx-NSs had a strong light absorption nature over a broad λ range [226]. MXene's dual-facet optical properties make it appealing for fluorescent probing: the previous uses MXene-QDs as luminophores for tunable photoinduced fluorescence emission, while the last uses the fluorescence resonance energy transfer effect given by the MXene-NSs to quench the luminous label [227]. MXene QDs' promise in fluorescence sensing was boosted by improvements in yield and doping method. Amino groups established to the surfaces of Ti3C2 MXene by passivating Ti3C2-QDs with polyethylenimine, n response to pH fluctuations would be pronate or depronate. The pH responsive Ti3C2 QDs exhibited a tunable luminous peak (460 nm) at different pHs due to photoabsorption electron localization, on the contrary to pH-insensitive fluorescent intensity of [Ru(dpp)3]Cl2 at 615 nm. Joining two fluorescent schemes, an MXene-based ratiometric PL sensor with minimal cytotoxicity and strong biocompatibility was proposed for intracellular pH sensing. However, utilizing two fluorescent systems made the biosensor setup more difficult.

Nitrogen-doped Ti3C2-QDs/DAP (2,3-diaminophenazine) NCs recently created a dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent sensor [228]. In the company of the H2O2, the fluorescence peak of N-doped Ti3C2-QDs at 448 nm quenched, whereas a new emission peak at 560 nm emerged at the same time (Fig. 11(e and f)). With high linearity and a LOD of 0.57 M, the ratio of these two fluorescence peaks was proportional to concentration of H2O2, giving an exciting biosensing platform for metabolic activity that includes H2O2 generation, for example xanthine oxidation [228]. Sensing systems founded on the MXene NSs' intrinsic fluorescence quenching action have been disclosed; with the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) effect achieved using MXene-fluorescence labeled DNA pairs. For example, using hydrogen bonding and metal chelation, a Cy3 tagged CD63 aptamer was preferentially absorbed on Ti3C2 [229]. The FERT effect quenched the fluorescence nanoprobe, but a particular combination of aptamer and exosomes dramatically restored Cy3 fluorescence. Ti3C2 MXene's persistent self-fluorescence signal at 650 nm allowed for a sensitive ratiometric fluorescence assay of exosomes with a low LOD of 1.4103-particles/mL. Also, Ti3C2Tx MXene functionalized with chimeric DNA has been used to detect dual cancer biomarkers [226]. The red fluorescence on cell membranes is triggered by hybridization of MCU1 (transmembrane glycoprotein mucin 1) and related aptamers in DNA, whereas identification of miR-21 and hairpin DNA leads in green fluorescence recovery in the cytoplasm. Nanoprobe achieved instantaneous in-situ mapping of MCF-7 breast cancer indicators in live cells thanks to MXene's exceptional biocompatibility. Aside from DNA probes, Ag+ and Mn2+ ions were shown to have a selective quenching effect on the Ti3C2-NSs [230]. Because of the exceptional average fluorescence decay period was reduced from 6.2ns to 1.1 and 1.2 ns, respectively, by interacting with Ag + or Mn2+ and functional groups on the MXene surface, making extremely tiny Ti3C2-NSs appealing for Mn2+ and Ag + sensing.

Because of their huge surface area and efficient charge transfer, coating 2D NMs on metal surfaces significantly boosts electromagnetic wave coupling for collective electron oscillations, enhancing the SPR effect. The SERS technology likewise uses SPR trend as a sensing mechanism, but NPs are needed to boost plasmon coupling and therefore Raman scattering intensity. Utilization of the hydrophilic yet high conductivity of MXene in design of the SPR or SERS platforms considerably increases the loading capacity of the biorecognition components that is significant for defeating the bottleneck of low target concentration sensitivity. Although both technologies convert bio-binding events at metal interfaces to optical signals, SPR sensing concentrates on changes in resonance angle, whereas the SERS assesses changes in the Raman scattering intensity [213].

Theoretical calculations found that when four layers of the T3C2Tx were deposited over Au-layer, the resonance angle shift rose from 0.685 to 0.800 when the refractive index change was set to 0.005 [231]. Ag, Cu, and Al layers all showed similar amplification, whereas four layers of T3C2Tx coated Au layer had the highest sensitivity of 160/RIU. By the way, a universal immunoassay biosensor was described using an Au-NPs modified Ti3C2 MXene nano-platform with the CNT-Ag-NPs conjugates as SPR enhancer [232]. The Au-NPs modified Ti3C2 has a rough, O2-rich surface that provides many binding sites for oriented antibody immobilization. For ultrasensitive CEA detection, a sandwich immuno approach was used to increase mass transfer on the Ti3C2 substrate and electromagnetic coupling in the Ag-NPs and Au-layers (carcinoembryonic antigen). As a consequence, the resonance angle shift was linearly related to the logarithm concentration of the CEA in the range of 2 × 1016M to 2 × 108 M (Fig. 11(g)). The 0.07 fM was determined to be the LOD.

One of the most significant obstacles for immunosensors is unstable non-covalent coupling of bio-detection components on the biosensing platform. So, the Ti3C2 MXene was more altered by the APTES for covalently immobilization of anti-CEA antibodies [233], in which multifunctional amino-functional group based Ti3C2 not only serves as a selective sensing platform for the CEA purpose, but also as an SPR enhancer when combined with hollow Au-NPs. The suggested biosensor has a low LOD of 0.15 fM and retained 80% of its activity after being kept for 28-days. MXene, in terms of the SERS nanoparticles, has an interesting in-situ assembly capability with the metal-NPs, thanks to its broad and negatively charged surface that is critical for the SERS intensity. Direct reduction of noble metal ions in the Ti3C2Tx colloid allowed for easy hybridization of Ag, Au, and Pd-NPs on the Ti3C2Tx substrate [234]. Prepared metal-NPs/MXene NCs improved methylene blue sensing sensitivity dramatically, with enhancement factors of 1.5 × 105, 1.17 × 105, and 9.61 × 104 for Ag, Au, and Pd-NPs, correspondingly. On the other hand, modifying a single kind of the NPs on MXene may not be sufficient to provide significant SERS signals for ultrasensitive analyte discovery. As a result, Au–Ag Janus NPs were studied further in a SERS based MXene biosensor that combines strong Au-NPs stability with substantial plasmonic effects of Ag-NPs [235]. In the presence of ochratoxin A, foreword of bimetallic NPs modified ochratoxin. Aptamer outcome in a massive electromagnetic field and intrinsic Raman signals that were attenuated. The Ti3C2Tx MXene, in contrast, showed a novel and stable Raman shift at 730 cm−1 that might serve up as a perfect internal standard. Therefore, ultrasensitive ratiometric detection of ochratoxin A with a LOD of 1.28pM (Fig. 11(h)) was possible. Furthermore [236],using Nb2C and Ta2C MXenes to improve SERS sensitivity because Nb5+ and Ta5+ have more excited electrons outside the nucleus, allowing for multi-coordination cations in SERS field. The Ta2C MXene's extraordinary SERS sensitivity allows it to identify SARS-CoV-2 spike protein with a LOD as low as 5 × 109 M.

MXene has a high capacity for absorption of light and absorbed light to convert into heat that has sparked attention in photothermal tumor ablation. MXene's energy band structure is substantially altered by tuning its surface terminations and TMs groups. As a consequence, surface alteration can improve the MXene's light absorption and conversion into heat method, allowing it to be used in the PTT and even photothermal immunoassay. NMs photothermal effect is used in photothermal immunoassays to convert light excitation into heat generation, whose quantity is proportional to amount of absorbed photothermal NMs on the surfaces. Photothermal NMs modified bio-recognition units, similar to the fluorescence labeled nano-probes, transform bio-binding events or reactions to instinctive and quantifiable readout into temperature rise. The need for complex detection gear is considerably reduced in this instance that is the fundamental benefit of the photothermal assay over fluorescence sensing [213]. Irradiation-absorption match and correct energy band configuration are two factors that are critical in increasing photothermal immunoassay sensing performance. The adsorption peak of the Ti3C2 MXene was found to reside in the 808 nm NIR region, while semiconductor-like MXene with a larger B.G had greater NIR absorption capacity [237]. The Ti3C2-QDs used as proficient light-heat converters in the combination with liposomes as signal amplifiers for high load encapsulation of Ti3C2-QDs to improve heat output for visible temperature increase [238]. Prostate-specific antigen (PSA) used as a paradigm target in a sandwich immunoassay format, and its preferential bio-binding resulted in immobilization of Ti3C2-QDs encapsulating liposomes on enzyme-linked immuno sorbent assay (ELISA) plate strips. A portable system with an 808 nm laser for absorption-matched excitation, the NIR image camera for temperature output footage, and a smart-phone for mapping and calibration was designed for easy photothermal measurement (Fig. 11(i)). Photothermal biosensor demonstrated exceptional chemical stability and a large lodging volume of the Ti3C2-QDs, resulting in reasonable sensitivity at the nanogram level, thanks to the logical encapsulation of liposome.

Fig. 11 (j–l) shows a strong linear relationship between temperature and the PSA concentrations below 80 ng/mL, with a LOD of about 0.4 ng/mL. A dual-mode probe based on the BP-QDs functionalized MXene described in both the ECL sensing and photothermal immunoassay of exosomes [239], in addition to the Ti3C2-QDs. Under light irradiation, the synergistic effects of BP-QDs and MXene NSs hybrids result in intense photothermal conversion and shorter electron transfer distance, considerably improving the detection sensitivity of both approaches. Exosomes logarithm concentration and increasing temperature showed excellent linearity in the photo-thermal test, with a LOD of 1.1 × 102particle.L−1. Although there have been few studies in this area, the existing photothermal immunoassay strategy has the advantages of simple excitation, readout, and sensitivity, allowing for the integration of a portable and down sized instrument. Furthermore, dual-signal readout in combination with a photothermal-amplified technique can improve electrochemical detection sensing performance, giving an alternate way of the point-of-care diagnostics based on MXene with increased sensitivity [240].

Cancer has been a significant life-threatening ailment for humans for centuries, and researchers are working hard to create new novel treatments. Cancer has far-reaching financial and emotional implications not just for patients but also for society as a whole. Cancer is one of the primary reasons for mortality in the world today [241]. According to a recent study, the yearly medical cost of cancer in the United States was expected to exceed $80.2 billion in 2015, and it is gradually growing. Traditional cancer treatment and diagnostic procedures include surgery, chemotherapy, radiation, targeted therapy, photoacoustic (PA) imaging, CT, and MRI [242,243]. Traditional cancer treatments, for example chemotherapy or radiation, have variety of drawbacks, such as limited selectivity or not-specific, which can result in a slew of adverse effects or unsuccessful treatment, which means it can also annihilate non-malignant cells. Surgery and radiation were utilized to treat solid tumors in 1960, but cure rates plateaued as a result of uncontrolled micro-metastases. In late 1960s, adoption of adjuvant chemotherapy transformed localized treatment that is an idea and has been around for a long time, resulting in important gains in the cure rates. Studying alterations in molecular of cancer cells improved rapidly after 1970. As a result, a plethora of novel drugs with a range of methods of action were created throughout 1980s. Innovation of the X-rays and radiation altered both therapeutic and diagnostic elements of cancer therapy, guide to progress of cancer treatment via cancer cell imaging (CT/MRI). From two decades, several therapeutics (hyperthermia, targeted cancer, gene therapies and photodynamic) based nanoscale and nanostructure and diagnostic agents were introduced. When compared to the traditional modalities, NPs demonstrated the following advantages;
1)
Small size allocates for EPR and simple infiltration through different cells and physiological barriers.
2)
Huge SSA permits for uncomplicated surface alteration and molecular functionalization to ensure biocompatibility, bioavailability and biodegradation, as well as the attachment of the cancer cell markers, and combining.

As a result, there is a pressing need for novel cancer therapy strategies to be developed. Using light-controlled treatments, for instance PTT, where photothermal agents are administered to cancer tissues and convert laser light energy into a heat, killing tumor cells [244], it is feasible to achieve an increased selectivity. Materials for the PTT should have an effective light-to-heat energy transfer, biocompatibility, and easy surface functionalization. Because visible light penetrates in tissue poorly, the NIR irradiation those match to ‘tissue optical windows’ or ‘biological windows are preferred’. Despite the availability of a variety of therapeutic and diagnostic approaches, their performance and efficiency are insufficient to offer complete satisfaction, making them inappropriate for selection. The efficiency is heavily influenced by the material used and how it is applied to the various modalities. Throughout the material selection process, the morphological configuration/shape, size, stability, active SSA, biocompatibility, electrical configuration, toxicity, magnetic properties, surface charge, surface plasmon, optical characteristics, and luminous qualities are all taken into account [245]. Due to their high active surface, 2D NSs display remarkable contacts with surface particular target moieties are a good alternative for the surface-active therapeutic agents in the drug administration and optical therapy. Recently, 2D NMs like, graphene [246], antimonene [247], BP [248], dichalcogenides and oxides [249] have effectively surpassed criteria for material selection and an excursion through the oncological region as biological agents [250]. The PTT has been shown to benefit from a wide range of NMs, including Au nanorods, copper sulfide NPs, BP, and carbon based NMs. MXenes are new PTT agents that have recently been introduced. Chemotherapy extends back to the early 1900s, when chemical treatments were utilized to eliminate particular types of malignant cells. MXenes were used in a range of medical uses. MXene's characteristics allow it to be used in bioimaging and anticancer treatments, especially employed as PTT agents.
