MXenes are also utilized as cargo transporters to create a synergistic therapeutic effect. MXene functions in therapeutics have so far been classified as follows:
(i)
Photothermal cell death
(ii)
ROS production for photodynamic cell killing
(iii)
Synergistic therapy cargo loading

Semiconducting MXenes were used for the ROS synthesis. MXenes themselves acted as photo-sensitizers for the PDT in the PTT/PDT/Chemo synergistic treatment employing MXene based materials because of electrical structure and photoelectronic properties that are unique of Ti3C2 NSs (Fig. 12 (a)). Using a ROS indicator dye, the capacity of the Ti3C2 NSs to form a ROS in irradiation was examined (1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran; DPBF; Fig. 12(b-i)). Energy transfer of photo-excited electrons from Ti3C2 to ground state of the O2 is thought to be responsible for the formation of 1O2, which is comparable to photodynamic performance of the BP and graphene based QDs. The Ti3C2's LSPR effect might possibly play a role in 1O2 production [119].

The ability of metals, particularly Au-NPs, to generate ROS when exposed to visible light is based on their LSPR effect, according to a previous study. For low-energy surface states and aggregated particles, energy transfer efficiency is extremely high. The large SSA of Ti3C2 NSs may be favorable for the LSPR. MXenes have been demonstrated to create enough ROS to efficiently kill cells. However, the mechanism behind this action is still unclear. As previously said, various plausible mechanisms have been proposed, but further study is required to confirm them. The photothermal impact of MXenes has sparked a lot of attention in terms of therapies.

Certain types of the MXenes, as previously indicated their photothermal conversion efficiency is good, and NIR absorption is substantial series, making them ideal for deep-tissue PTT. Following 808 nm laser irradiation, such as, Ti3C2, Nb2C, and Ta4C3 were effectively confirmed for in-vivo PTT of tumors, with outstanding photothermal conversion efficiency of 30.6-percent, 36.5%, and 34.9%, respectively [112]. More intriguingly, when exposed to a 1064 nm laser, Nb2C displayed a photo-thermal conversion efficiency of 46.65%, allowing for ultra-deep tissue PTT [145]. Ultra-small MXene QDs have been found to have a greater photothermal conversion efficiency (52.2-percent of Ti3C2 MXene QDs vs., 30.6-percent of Ti3C2 bigger MXene NFs [112], despite the fact that characterization parameters were dissimilar. This evaluation demonstrated that the scraped MXenes are very proficient for the PTT, most likely as to increased heat generation due to more defects in scraped MXene QDs. MXenes can be utilized as drug carriers in cancer therapy in addition to destroying cells directly by the PDT or PTT. The MXenes flat surface can be tailored or conjugated with a number of functional/therapeutic cargos. Dong et al. [119], for example, employed the DOX, a positively charged chemo-drug, binds to the Ti3C2 MXene negative surface. The negatively charged tumor-targeting HA was then put LBL on top of the DOX to create a LBL configuration. The DOX-induced chemotherapy was paired with Ti3C2 MXenes' intrinsic PDT/PTT to produce a synergistic treatment. Chen et al. [251], discovered extremely high drug-loading capacity of DOX (211.8-percent) Ti3C2 MXenes modified with the SP. Significantly, this method shows pH-responsive and the NIR laser-triggered on-demand drug release. MXenes may transport a variety of payloads in addition to medications. MnOx-loaded MXenes, for example were shown to be effective as imaging contrast agents by Chen et al., MXenes may also be dyed using 12,13 Dyes, which makes them appealing for energy transfer applications. MXenes have the potential to be a versatile carrier for a wide range of cargos in multimodal imaging, treatments, and theranostics.

Xuan et al. [252], described a method for synthesizing the Ti3C2Tx MXenes with NIR absorbance that are terminated with Al oxy-anion. In contradiction of traditional synthetic approaches, the synthesis produced NSs ending with the terminations like OH or F that were revealed to be excellent photothermal agents against murine breast cancer cells [253]. Dai et al. [133], created MnOx/Ti3C2 NCs for PTT and bioimaging, but compared to the Ti3C2 NSs it has a poorer photothermal conversion efficiency. Although they effectively kill cancer cells in-vitro after the SP surface modification, and they also concealed the formation of the murine-breast tumors in-vivo after the IR laser irradiation. Yu et al. [148], conducted an additional research on this subject, presenting a easy and secure F-free technique for preparing Ti3C2 QDs for the PTT. Synthesized Ti3C2 QDs had one of utmost efficiencies of photothermal conversion in MXene materials. Their biocompatibility was confirmed through an in-vitro and in-vivo research, and after intravenous injection into mice, the obtained NSs killed cervical cancer cells at a minor concentration than the Ti3C2-SP formed by conventional way [112]. Furthermore, the Ti3C2 MXenes required a lower laser power density for tumor ablation when in contrast to earlier research. To perform a PTT procedure, A. Szuplewska et al. [254], used Ti-based MXene with diverse stoichiometric quantities of Ti2C. On non-malignant and cancerous cell lines resulting from two human cells, potential for photothermal agents made from nanostructures was tested. Researchers were able to compare similarity of NFs for various types of cells using this method, as well as investigate the selectivity of the MXenes for malignant cells. The Ti2C worked well in the NIR-induced based killing of cancer tissues.

The MXenes had little effect on the viability of normal cells after expose to NMs, at comparatively elevated concentration (∼35 mg/ml). The material's proclivity to accumulate preferentially in cancerous cells, as verified by cellular uptake analysis by the SEM and confocal microscopy, could explain this effect. Furthermore, Ti2C-PEG had higher photothermal conversion efficiency than other Ti-based nanostructures. Also Ti-based MXenes and other MXene compounds were studied useful in anticancer therapy. H. Lin et al. [125], investigated the PTT ability of a novel Ta4C3 MXene NSs. The Ta4C3 demonstrated higher photothermal conversion efficiency than the Ti3C2 MXene, as earlier studied one. After surface alteration with SP, they also confirmed excellent photothermal stability and colloidal stability in physiological media. Furthermore, they found that Ta4C3-SP had a longer blood circulation half-life than the Ti3C2-SP, and that it accumulated at a lower level in tumors due to difference in the NPs size and EPR effect. C. Dai et al. [131], presented one more study of tantalum-based MXenes, using in-situ reaction similar to that applied to make Ti3C2 MXene to make MnOx/Ta4C3. Tantalum-based NCs had good photothermal conversion efficiency than the earlier studied MnOx/Ti3C2. Furthermore, capability to applied MnOx/Ta4C3 in a variety of bioimaging system is undeniable. The majority of PTT agents, including the MXene examples explained above, have a peak in primary biological window (NIR-I). The NIR-II, on the other hand, has a higher MPE than the NIR-I that relates to highest secure laser power density for human skin exposure. H. Lin et al. [145], studied Nb2C for PTT in both biological window areas. The PVP was applied to the surface of Nb2C NSs to make sure the durability and biocompatibility in physical condition. The Nb2C demonstrated good photothermal conversion efficiencies at 808 nm and 1064 nm. The PTT materials should be able to localize at tumor sites while also efficiently converting light to heat.

According to some studies, NPs accumulation in tumors is lower than in other organs for instance, liver and spleen. As a result, to be therapeutic, innovative photothermal agents must have adequate tumor growth as well as good biocompatibility to check harming healthy tissues in the absence of irradiation. The ICP-OES and confocal microscopy revealed that the Nb2C-PVP MXenes achieved have a tendency to accumulate in cancer tissues (FITC-labeled Nb2C-PVP). MXene in-vivo tumor accumulation was 2.2-percent of injected dose, according to the Inductively Coupled Plasma Optical Emission Spectroscopy (ICP-OES) and Thermogravimetry Analysis (TGA), and its half-life in the circulatory system was 1 h and 20-min. The PTT was highly effective in both biological window regions [255], and the photothermal agents used in the PTT were simply excreted from the body.

The bio-distribution and excretion routes of the NPs in the organism are influenced by size; chemical structure, surface charge, and dosage are all factors to consider for surface alteration, and time. H. Lin et al. [256], investigated the biodegradation of the Nb2C-PVP as well. The researchers used human-myelo-peroxidase to the biodegrade Nb2C NSs; humans have a naturally occurring enzyme that can degrade carbon based molecules. After treating NSs with the enzyme for 24 hs in H2O2, ex-vivo biodegradation was observed such as the NSs were shrunk in size, their shape changed, and the absorbency of the solution reduced. Furthermore, the bio-degradation was observed in an in-vivo experiment, in which mice excreted nearly 20% of the introduced Nb2C-PVP 48 h after injection [256]. The PTT has recently been tested in conjunction with other anticancer treatments like, chemotherapy and PDT [257]. In combined therapies, synergistic effects have been observed that takes place when combined efficacy of two or more shared therapies is superior to separate cures. This resulted in lower chemotherapy drug doses, lower photothermal agent and photo-sensitizer concentrations, and lower laser powers for PTT and PDT, all of resulted in smaller amount of negative consequences and better cure outcomes. Many photothermal agents, including Au nano-rods, Cu2-xS NPs [258], and carbon nanospheres [259], show promising results when shared with other antitumor treatment methods. MXenes have also been studied in combination therapy. G. Liu et al. [119], demonstrated a tumor-targeting based on the Ti3C2 nano-platform for the synergistic PDT, PTT, and chemotherapy. The Ti3C2 showed a high photoconversion rate, therefore the resultant MXene was loaded with DOX via layer-by-layer (LBL) adsorption for the chemotherapy (DOX cargo efficiency was calculated spectro-photometrically to be 84-percent). Also passive tumor accumulation via EPR, authors' attained active tumor targeting by coating MXene with the HA that binds to cancer cells that express CD44. Nanoplatforms are liable to create a high level of ROS after HA degradation. Because DOX or Ti3C2-DOX alone resulted in only limited tumor development reserve was detected, and Ti3C2 and DOX dosages used were minimal, a synergistic effect was demonstrated following an in-vivo PTT by Ti3C2-DOX87. Several MXene-based materials were studied for the PTT, bioimaging, and diagnosis; however, some MXenes necessitate low doses or use of high-power lasers. Furthermore, research to see when and how MXenes are detoxified, as well as their long-standing effects on the living organisms is lacking. Given that all MXenes appear to cause hyperthermia, such research is essential, and it will help distinguish different MXenes and aid in the selection of the best material. Method of action and influence of MXenes also required looking into the single-cell level, but only one study has done so. According to Jastrze'bska et al. [260], high value of Ti3C2 MXene be liable to generate ROS in skin and lung cells. Calculated oxidative stress phenomenon has been planned as fundamental method of action of MXenes. Table 4 shows a summary of MXenes therapeutic characteristics results.

Due to the wide range of cancer types and patient individuality, mono-therapy encounters several challenges such medication resistance, severe side effects, and protracted treatment cycles. It is very challenging to predict the best effectiveness and full recovery. Combination therapy, a novel development in cancer treatment, makes clinical applications more realistic in this area. However, creating a multifunctional system necessitates a challenging design process and a lengthy synthesis process. Therefore, innovative combination therapy has gained popularity recently.

Theranostics is a new discipline of nanomedicine that the aims to combine diagnostic and therapeutic capabilities in single device. Theranostic medications offer early diagnosis of sickness by increasing contrast in many imaging modalities and giving personalized therapy. Special imaging techniques can be used to assess theranostic drug absorption, distribution, metabolism, and excretion (ADME), allowing for increased therapeutic efficacy and fewer side effects. The 2D NMs ranging in size from 1 to 100 nm, appropriate for drug delivery systems. Because NMs are smaller in size than larger molecules, they may circulate more freely inside human body. Furthermore, routine pharmaceutical administration needs large drug dosages to attain therapeutic levels that results in toxicity to normal cells and tissues as well as drug resistance. This justifies the use of nano-medicines as delivery systems for the medications encapsulated or connected to particular tissues. Because of their 2D planar configuration and novel physicochemical properties that facilitate drug loading for precision treatment, MXenes have the potential to be effective drug delivery platforms. Modified ultrathin Ti3C2 NSs may easily pass through blood vessels [112], and their high SSA enables proficient coating of the anticancer drug DOX and large drug releasing value in acidic settings, resulting in tumor eradication [268]. The NIR-irradiation at 808 nm increased drug release by 23.5-percent in very acidic circumstances [268]. MXenes have the drug release platforms as a result of the 2D planar structure and unique physicochemical features that permit drug loading for precise therapy. Modified ultrathin Ti3C2 NSs can readily flow through blood arteries [112], and their enormous SSA allows for effective coating of the anticancer DOX and high drug-releasing proportions in non-basic environments, guiding for tumor elimination [268].

In extremely acidic conditions, the NIR-irradiation at 808 nm boosted drug release by 23.5-percent [268]. The extraordinary drug absorption in MXene-NCs based hydrogel aids the hydrogen-bonding interactions in H2O and MXenes' hydrophilic surface functional groups and polymer chains' hydrophilic groups (-CONH2). Ti3C2/PAM hydrogels expand for significantly longer time than BIS/PAM hydrogels, indicating a greater drug loading capacity [269]. In addition, as compared to BIS/PAM hydrogels, Ti3C2/PAM hydrogels had a drug releasing percentage of 62.1–81.4% (45-percent). Numerous kinds of MXenes has shown significant potential in theranostics. Such as, Ti3C2 and Nb2C, were employed for simultaneous PA images and PTT caused by their excellent photothermal conversion capacities [131]. Also, Chen et al. [131], studied a loading of MnOx on Ti3C2 for multimodal (MR/CT/PA) imaging-guided PTT of malignancies (Fig. 13 (a)). Ta4C3 MXenes were produced using a biocompatible Ta-element with a high Z of 73, as practical metal, resulting in improved X-ray/CT contrast for CT-imaging. They performed a novel MRI/CT/PA tri-modal imaging-guided PTT in NIR-I window (Fig. 13(b-e)), taking use of Ta4C3 MXenes have a high photothermal conversion efficiency, and loaded MnOx has an extra MRI characteristic. Fig. 14 (a) shows imaging-guided PTT using MXenes in NIR-II window [256]. The Nb2C exhibits strong photothermal conversion in the NIR-I and II windows (Fig. 14(b and c)), making it a feasible option for extremely efficient PA imaging-guided PTT to tumor in NIR-I and II windows (Fig. 14(d)).

In-vivo PA/CT dual-modal imaging-guided PTT employing both i. v and i. m injections of SP-coated Ta4C3 MXenes has recently been developed. According to their findings [125], Ta4C3 MXenes were biocompatible after SP-coating and an accumulated proficiently inside tumor through EPR effect after intravenous delivery. Intravenous injection of such drugs resulted in PTT efficiency and imaging capabilities equivalent to intratumoral administration. Chen et al., 49 recently coated Ta4C3 MXenes with iron oxide and demonstrated CT/MR images in NCs. Ta4C3-iron oxide NCs showed competent breast-cancer theranostics using CT/MR dual-modal images, which directed by the PTT after surface modification with the SP [270]. Magnetic NPs have recently been used into drug delivery systems in order to increase nano-carrier (NCs) controllability, particularly in magnetic fields [271,272]. One example is the formation of a Ti3C2-CoNWs metal-semiconductor heterojunction by combining Co-NWs and the Ti3C2 NSs. The drug loading efficiency of Ti3C2 based NCs heterojunction were improved by increasing DOX-NCs ratios. At the greatest mass ratio of 3, DOX/Ti3C2-CoNWs NCs hetero-junction had a maximum drug loading capacity of 225.05-percent that is superior to the single Ti3C2 NSs (84-percent) and most spherical drug delivery NCs (10–30%) [119,273]. In acidic circumstances, the solubility and hydrophilicity of DOX drug amplified, leading drug release to be increased [274]. Furthermore, the NIR irradiation of DOX-loaded NCs augmented amount of medicine released [275]. In compared to conventional spherical systems, bare Ti3C2 MXene were demonstrated to be much superior for drug release, with a >50% increase in drug release percentage. Ti3C2 Drug release percentages improved by at least 30% and 200-percent when NSs were treated with hydrogel or magnetic semiconductors correspondingly. As a consequence, Ti3C2 demonstrates a promising drug delivery NCs for cancer therapy and precision drug delivery that may be considerably superior to current nano-based drug delivery systems, whether pure or modified. Table 5 summarized different kind of MXenes and their uses in cancer therapy.

The MXenes' usual 2D planar architecture gives them a high SSA, making it simple to attach variety of therapeutic agents on the surface of layered structures, much like other 2D nanoplatforms, inorganic mesoporous carriers, and conventional organic nanoplatforms. Given the strong photothermal conversion efficiency of 2D Ti3C2 MXenes, it was anticipated that these materials would also be used for very effective tumor ablation by a combination of DOX-loaded chemotherapy and synergistic MXene-assisted photothermal eradication [280]. The continuously photothermal ablation of tumors and the procedure of hyperthermia-enhanced drug distribution may be ensured by the as-yielded Ti3C2 MXenes' favorable photothermal stability during the four-cycle processes of heating and cooling. The Ti3C2 MXenes as a drug delivery nano-platform, in particular, not only have a high drug loading capacity of 211.8%, but also allow for pH-responsive and NIR laser-triggered drug release. Additionally, CLSM was utilized to show that the combination of Ti3C2-promoted PTT and chemotherapy led to the death of nearly all 4T1 cells. On 4T1 tumor-bearing mice models, it is noteworthy that the combination of Ti3C2 assisted PTT and chemotherapy led to full tumor eradication without recurrence in the treatment period, demonstrating the desired synergistic effect of PTT and chemot-herapy.

For oncologic intervention, PDT offers the benefits of minimum invasiveness and spatiotemporal specificity. Importantly, the excited photosensitizer (PS) will convert its excited-state energy to molecular oxygen species for the generation of ROS, which will cause tumor-cell death following the oxidation of the primary cellular components [281,282]. A synergistic treatment combining chemotherapy and phototherapy might be produced by merging cargo delivery with phototherapeutic modalities like PTT and PDT. It is strongly anticipated that the concurrent PTT/PDT/chemotherapy-based multitherapeutic modality will be introduced by the MXene-based nanotherapeutic agent in order to achieve the highly effective synergistic therapies. Layer-by-layer surface modification of Ti3C2 NSs with DOX as chemotherapeutic treatment and hyaluronic acid (HA) as the tumor-targeting agent, a novel MXene NMs based nano-platform was built as a paradigm [119]. This Ti3C2-DOX multifunctional nanoplatform, which is based on Ti3C2 NSs, has a maximum drug loading capacity of 84.2%. As anticipated, the Ti3C2-DOX demonstrates an excellent pH-responsive drug-releasing behavior (in moderate acidity), taking advantage of the HA shell's efficient protection in a neutral environment. In meantime, the addition of NIR laser-induced photothermal effect of Ti3C2 (temperature up to 50 °C) should improve the acid TME's ability to transport drugs more effectively. Following the intracellular endocytosis of Ti3C2-DOX, the photothermal effect for hyperthermia and the photodynamic process for ROS production were induced using an 808 nm NIR laser. It is very appealing that, in addition to the laborious creation of material structure, efficient synergistic therapeutic outcome has been attained by the simple therapeutic drug loading on 2D MXenes NMs, offering a model of synergistic tumor therapy based on additional 2D nano-systems.

A potential method for increasing the accumulation of drug carriers or therapeutic substances toward the disease location is active targeting. The surface of MXenes, which are synthesized with RGD-targeted Ti3C2@mMSNs-RGD, has undergone nano-pore engineering. This has given MXenes' synergistic multi-therapies, a well defined mesoporous nanostructure for drug release and delivery, tumor-specificity for active targeting responses, and inherent photothermal conversion performance for PTT [283]. The RGD-targeted photothermal-ablation effectiveness of Ti3C2@mMSNs-RGD under NIR laser demonstrated that more hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) cells were destroyed by Ti3C2@mMSNs-RGD than by Ti3C2@mMSNs-PEG without RGD modification when the laser energy was increased. Due to the active-targeting ability between RGD peptides anchored on Ti3C2@mMSNs surface and αvβ3 ligands expressed on HCC cell membranes, Ti3C2@mMSNs-RGD simultaneously displayed obvious superiority in the suppressing HCC cells. This allowed for more synergistic therapeutic agents to target the HCC cells via an effective endocytosis, as demonstrated by CLSM. Systematic in vivo assessments additionally showed that Ti3C2@mMSNs-RGD had a high active-targeting outcome (contributed by RGD) into tumor, and that synergistic chemotherapy (generated from MSNs) and hyperthermia (contributed by Ti3C2 MXene) had completely eliminated the tumor without recurrence.

Many 2D-NMs have been examined for their potential antibacterial properties using diverse techniques. Growth of microbiological species, for instance, S. cerevisiae, S. aureus, and Escherichia coli was considerably reduced following addition of the Zn–Ti LDHs to bacterium solution due to the influence of the LDH size and the production of the ROS by Ti3+ in the visible light [284]. Antibacterial characteristics of chemically exfoliated MoS2 (ce-MoS2) were examined by introducing a ce-MoS2 solution to a bacterial culture [285]. As previously stated that the semiconducting nature of the MXenes possibly useful in destruction of bacterial cells. MXenes might use reactive metal-F couples to transfer reactive electrons to cell membranes, similar to how semiconductors produce electrons and holes, leading in the type-I PDT-like cell death [286]. This impact is verified in the subsequent antibiotic study. K. Rasool et al. [286], studied Ti3C2 in antibacterial death of Escherichia coli and Bacillus subtilis cells (Fig. 15 (a)).

To calculate their ability to inhibit bacterial growth, Ti3C2 MXenes were grown with bacteria cells for 4 hs. Ability of the MXenes to strongly limit bacterial cultivation was demonstrated from culture plate, determining the total the number of colonies following with dealing a various concentrations of MXenes (0–200 mg/ml of Ti3C2) (Fig. 15(b)). According to their findings, MXenes caused oxidative stress on bacterial cell membranes. The ROS dependent/independent oxidative stressors were studied in two different abiotic assays. They hypothesized that MXenes acted as an antibacterial agent that cause cell death by establishing a conductive bridge across an insulating lipid bilayer, allowing electron transfer from bacterial internal components to outside atmosphere. Because of their anionic nature, negative charge appears at the Ti3C2 NSs surfaces. Furthermore, the Ti3C2 high hydrophilic nature can increase bacterial interaction with surface, ensuing to inactivate adhering micro-organisms by directly in contact-killing method. In addition, in the oxygen groups of the Ti3C2 MXenes and lipo-polysaccharide strings of cell membrane hydrogen bonding may limit the food absorption, inhibiting bacterial growth in a similar way as the GO NSs, but with greater efficacy owing to the MXenes' higher conductivity. MXenes' antibacterial properties have lately been employed to prevent the bio-fouling in long-term water/wastewater treatment [289]. They coated the Ti3C2 MXenes on a PVDF support as antibacterial membrane (Fig. 15(c-e)), which indicates a 70% inhabitants compromised and dead cells after 24 hs exposure from the E. coli and Bacillus subtilis than controlled the PVDF support (Fig. 15(f-h)). Surface-oxidized Ti3C2 MXenes displayed significantly improved antibacterial activity as compared to as synthesized the Ti3C2 MXenes. As a result, MXenes have a great deal of potential in antibacterial research.

Conventional organic materials with elevated biocompatibility/biodegradability were investigated for biomedical uses; however, their pitiable thermal/chemical stability and solitary functional property was a shortcoming preventing clinical development [290,291]. Due to inherent properties for instance, easy functionalization, tunable configuration, advantageous biocompatibility, specific biodegradation, multi-functionality, and physiological durability, inorganic MXene-based NMs have a high clinical translation potential. It is difficult for most organic entities to achieve these specific characteristics. Despite the fact that the MXenes have novel characteristics in biomedical applications, the bio-safety of the rising MXenes family decides their future clinical potential. MXene's cytotoxic effects, on the other hand, remained a big concern. As a result, prior to its introduction into the human physiological system, MXene must undergo extensive biocompatibility testing. Numerous in-vitro and in-vivo toxicological studies were published on MXene interactions with living animals like, microbes, mammalian cells, and animal models. The physicochemical characteristics of MXene impacted the reported biological response and cytotoxicity. MXene's biocompatibility and cytotoxicity were impacted by size, dose, and surface coating.

Effects of biomedicine derived from critical parameters of the 2D NMs, like, solubility, dispersing ability, enduring toxicity, and biodegradability are not very well known; however, toxicity/bio-compatibility evaluations of various types of the 2D NMs are ongoing [292]. The cytotoxicity of the MXene is influenced by its functional groups (F, O, and OH), size (1–100 nm), oxidative state (Ti2O3), manufacturing procedures, type and dose administration, and exposure periods (24–48 hs). Cells may consume MXene with sizes ranging from 1 to 100 nm via the endocytosis process. At doses of up to 250 mg/L, MXene produces cytotoxicity when cell viability falls below 70%. When functional surface termination groups (F, OH, and O) are introduced to the cell surface membrane, they may connect to the available cationic sites, threatening the cell membrane's integrity. Oxidized MXene synthesis processes can result in varying degrees of oxidizing residue (Ti2O3) in the end product, ensuing in cell membrane rupturing. After two days of injection, MXene is progressively eliminated from the mice's body through urine and feces in amounts of 18.7% and 10.35-percent, respectively. On a zebra-fish embryo model, potential ecotoxicological studies of the MXene NMs in-vivo on aquatic biota were done in a recent study, with the goal of indirectly revealing the potential human health harmful effects as a result of the toxicological effect.

Number of neurons in the spinal cord area of Ti3C2Tx-treated zebra-fish embryos was comparable to that of the DMSO negative control (Fig. 16 (a)), indicating that Ti3C2Tx had no adverse toxicological effect on the neuron and muscle activity of zebra-fish embryo model at the NOEC (50 g/mL) (Fig. 16(b and c)). As the LC50 of Ti3C2Tx exceeds 100 g/mL, Ti3C2Tx may be classified as ‘practically harmless’ by Fish and Wildlife Service's (FWS) Acute Toxicity Rating Scale. In addition, significant progress has been made in demonstrating their potential for great biocompatibility in biomedicine. Pristine MXenes and their NCs have been shown to exhibit little cytotoxicity toward cells. After 48 hs of coincubation, the Ti3C2-SP NSs have no effect on the viability of the 4T1 cell line, even at a high concentration of 400g/mL [112]. The in-vitro toxicity of the SP-Ta4C3 on cells was assessed using a standard Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) assay. The SP-Ta4C3 had no influence on survival of the 4T1 cell line even at doses of up to 400 g/mL (Fig. 16(d)) [145]. Furthermore, Nb2C-PVP was incubated with two cancer cell lines; glioma U87 and breast 4T1, with no apparent suppressive effect on either cell line, even at high concentrations (200 g/mL) (Fig. 16(e)) [145]. After 48 hs of coincubation with the NCs of MnOx/Ti3C2-SP, a negligible cytotoxicity was initiated, yet at a elevated concentration of 160 g/mL, revealing the comparably great bio-compatibility of the MnOx/SP-Ta3C2 NC NSs [133].

In-vivo testing of the potential bio-safety and biocompatibility of surface tailored MXene against mice was also performed. After receiving three doses of surface-modified MXenes, healthy Kunming mice were examined after 1, 7, and 28-days (Nb2C-PVP dosage of 20 mg/kg) [125]. There was no variation in mouse behavior between the three treatment groups (NIR-I, NIR-II, and daylight exposure) and control group, and no irregularity in mouse body weights was seen throughout the duration of three observation periods. Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) staining examinations of main organs (spleen, liver, heart, kidney and lung) following a 28-day diet revealed no evident acute, chronic pathological toxicity, or deleterious effects when comparing the control and treatment groups (Fig. 16(f)). All of hematological indices and biochemical indicators in the plasma tests were normal (Fig. 17 (a–c)). These findings imply that the Nb2C-PVP may be biocompatible for future in-vivo tumor theranostics therapeutic techniques. The same studies were performed on Ti3C2 and Ta4C3 MXenes, both of which demonstrated minimal cytotoxicity and high in-vivo bio-compatibility.

Given the current findings, additional study and optimization of in-vivo and in-vitro toxicity evaluations, for instance reproductive toxicity [294], or genotoxicity [292] are required to fully harness the potential of the MXene-based theranostic nano-platform for a wide variety of the medicinal applications. MXene's toxicity to mammalian cells is currently thought to be related to oxidative stress, which leads to apoptosis. Although, disruption methods of MXene NPs to cells were only briefly discussed, and it is also very critical to understand particular signaling pathways at a deeper level. Modifying the surface characteristics of MXene by conjugating it with biopolymers is one strategy for reducing its cytotoxicity. Coatings such as PEG, PLGA, Chitosan, Poly-l-Lysine, collagen, and silk fibroin lower MXene's toxicity while increasing its biocompatibility. Machine learning algorithms were used to properly calculate the cytotoxicity of MXene chemicals produced in the lab. This strategy might decrease the number of cytotoxicity tests required in the future and the number of failures. This review has offered critical data for recent findings about the potential dangers or safe doses of MXene-based products for biological applications [295].

While more MXenes will surely be discovered, it is vital to focus on the fundamental challenges that the MXenes community has yet to completely address. The key goals for extending the field's growth and moving toward practical applications were environmentally friendly, safe, efficient, and scalable synthesis procedures, surface chemistry control, chemical stability enhancement, and physical property understanding. Majority of MXenes are presently synthesized using top-down processes that lack control over experimental circumstances and subsequent products. There are just few attempts at bottom-up synthesis processes for MXenes have been performed thus far, and SL MXenes have yet to be synthesized using this method. As a result, further study should be done in this area, as well as research into new synthetic processes that might be utilized to construct MXenes with customizable morphology, topologies, and surface terminations. Furthermore, as comparison to enormous number of the MXenes predicted theoretically, only a few MXenes were produced in the laboratory. Even though many other types of the MXenes with attractive properties have been predicted, a few of them, like magnetic Cr-contained MXenes have yet to be experimentally generated. As a result, greater effort should be placed into producing MXenes, particularly those with distinguishing characteristics. A major focus is focused on the health and environmental safety/toxicity of MXenes with different TMs and surface terminations. The majority of elementary researches on production optimization and the effect of production and processing variables on MXene surface chemistry and properties have employed Ti3C2Tx. Systematic studies of the effect of production and processing circumstances on structure, surface chemistry, and characteristics of MXenes with varying M, X, and n will be tremendously beneficial in expanding our understanding for these materials.

MXene QDs, for example, exhibit luminescence properties that ordinary MXene NF analogs do not have. MXene QDs have been synthesized, alternatively in a complex process. At the moment, very small MXene QDs are generated following a succession of centrifugations, the larger particles are filtered away, resulting in a wide size range fluctuations, unpredictable shape, and limited reproducibility. As a result, efficient MXene QDs synthesis strategies, for instance, bottom-up methods are much desired. MXenes with morphologies other than flat films, for instance nanotubes and nanocages, should also be investigated. Furthermore, by mixing MXenes with other types of functional materials to create hybrid materials that have the best of both materials properties, allowing for multifunction biological applications. To fully understand the properties of the MXenes, precise control of their sizes, components, and surface coatings is necessary. Despite the fact that multiple computations utilized to reveal characteristics of the MXenes, those qualities which are intimately linked to biological functions have yet to be thoroughly investigated. In the majority of cases, experiments on MXenes with heterogeneous surface terminations were carried out. Because of the difficulties in controlling their terminations, the MXenes properties with and without functional groups have not yet been studied.

Despite the fact that the MXenes have distinct properties and have demonstrated considerable potential for therapeutic uses, clinical translation remains a challenge. The long-standing bio-safety of the MXenes has yet not been properly researched. Numerous investigations indicated that presently biocompatible MXenes are accessible for the biomedical purposes, and a few are even biodegradable in-vivo [119], regardless of that the majority of such investigations were based on the cell experiments or short-term hematological testing. For future biological applications, a systematic investigation of MXene bio-safety will be crucial. In healthy Balb/c nude mice, Chen et al. [296], investigated the metabolism and excretion of Ti3C2 coated with the SP. They observed that 48 hs after injection, Ti3C2 coated SP was steadily flushed out of the body via urine and feces at rates of 18.70% and 10.35%, respectively [296]. Using the zebra-fish embryo model, another recent study looked at Ti3C2 biocompatibility. In Ti3C2 absorption by zebra-fish embryos there was no substantial teratogenic effects that were dose-dependent at 100 mg/mL Ti3C2 under ambient conditions. Ti3C2 may be classified as ‘practically harmless’ by the Fish and Wildlife Service's (FWS) Acute Toxicity Rating Scale after a series of acute toxicity, locomotion, and neuro-toxicity tests [297]. Overall, further study on the MXenes' protracted toxicity, biocompatibility, immunology, pharmacology, and bio-distribution in minor animal models (such as zebra fish and mice) is required (e.g. dog, monkey and pig).

Furthermore, recent MXene theoretical simulations have focused on their electrical properties for energy evolution rather than their optical features that are important for medical applications. Theoretical research should pay a higher emphasis on optical properties in order to fore see optimum MXenes for biological fields. These computations studies should consider, except not only restricted to study of MXenes' optical absorption capabilities and tuning methods for example doping neighboring ions and modifying surface terminations. It should also compute MXene QDs emission that is governed by material B. Gs, composition, and surface terminations. Using proposed estimates, MXene emission should be set towards the NIR band, which is advantageous for the deep-tissue studies. Few modern programs' methodologies are also questionable. MXenes ROS production for example, remains unknown. A better understanding of how MXenes work might lead the creation of the MXenes with enhanced PDT efficiency. Exploration of new MXene conditions for biological applications is another field of investigation. MXenes, for example established significant photothermal properties in the NIR-II window. In NIR-II window PA-images, on the other hand is still a work in progress. PA-images at the NIR-II window would be favored for biomedical uses because of increased tissue penetration depth of NIR-II light compared to NIR-I light. More applications based on MXenes' SERS capabilities, as well as the optimal MXene composition for SERS, should be researched. While compared to light, magnetic field has limitless diffusion intensity. Deep-tissue non-invasive theranostics employing a magnet as stimulus to make active magnetic MXenes for magneto-thermal and MRI applications might be achievable. Likewise, MXenes responses to ultrasonography have yet to be investigated. Ultrasonography may be utilized to efficiently trigger MXenes in a range of biological applications since particular MXenes have a tiny B.G and a broad lateral dimension. Heavy metal-based MXenes, for instance W/Ta MXenes, may be beneficial for nuclear/CT imaging, whereas MXenes containing radioactive metals should be acceptable for PET. Common catalytic properties of MXenes might also be employed to catalyze specific biological processes in bio-systems. In general biological utilizations will rely heavily on MXene-based materials.

Over the last 10 years, the production of M5C4Tx, i-MXenes (with/without di-vacancies in their basal planes), o-MXenes, and different solid solutions has expanded the domain of 2D TMs carbides and nitrides. Despite the fact that only about 50 MXenes have been identified so far, atomistic design has demonstrated control over their physical, mechanical, and electrochemical properties. Composition-dependent optical properties of solid solution MXenes have been shown. Surface chemistry has been used to achieve reversible change of the work function, plasmon resonance, and Fermi level for distinct MXenes. Regarding to the biological applications, MXenes offer intriguing electrical, optical, and magnetic properties that may be tailored for a variety of innovative biological uses. Using a number of approved preparation procedures, suitable MXenes can be synthesized and employed for certain biological goals. MXenes' remarkable functions established in biomedical utilizations for instance biosensors, antimicrobial materials, bioimaging probes, and therapeutic/theranostics treatments. More emphasis should be placed on the design and production of new MXenes for future biological applications.

It's gratifying to observe that some of the issues raised by the scientific community less than a year ago have already been resolved. Adding antioxidants or capping the edges of MXene, the oxidation/degradation stability of Ti3C2Tx was increased, and a shelf life of up to a year in dilute aqueous solution at RT has been achieved by synthesizing extremely stoichiometric MXene. Understanding how stoichiometry affects environmental stability should lead to more stable MXenes of diverse compositions. Regardless of these remarks, the long-term efficacy of various oxidation mitigation techniques is uncertain. This is especially true in situations where water is present and exposed to the environment. V. Kamysbayev et al. [298], used molten salt synthesis and termination exchange to produce many novel surface terminations. MXenes with high entropy have been discovered; new halogen synthesis procedures have been devised employing salt solutions without acid additions, as well as I2, Br2, and other halogens. Superconductivity with a termination has also been shown. Isocyanates changed the surface chemistry of MXene, making it hydrophobic. MXene becomes organophilic and dispersible in non-polar liquids by exchanging Li from in-between layers with di(hydrogenated tallow) benzyl methyl ammonium chloride [69].

In many applications, intercalation is critical for MXene processing, characteristics, and performance. However, nothing is known about intercalants in confinement, their interactions with catalytically active MXene NSs, and their interactions with solvents (e.g. confined water). Intercalation in Ti3C2Tx has received the most attention. Fundamental and systematic research is required to get a better knowledge of intercalation in MXenes, which will allow for correct correlation of intercalated MXenes' composition, structure, characteristics, and behavior in many applications [69].

While carbide MXene manufacturing has advanced, nitride synthesis has lagged. Vapor phase synthesis is necessary to incorporate MXenes on chips using existing nanostructured device methods. If large-scale, environmental friendly synthesis procedures are established, MXenes will be widely employed hereafter as additive manufacturing equipments. With careful control of configuration and surface chemistry e.g. defects and strain engineering, inherently semiconducting, topologically insulating, and ferromagnetic MXenes, with additional discoveries in the MXene physics and chemistry, should be achievable. MXenes, which are physically strong, environmentally stable, and electrically conductive, might have a significant impact on flexible, printable, and wearable self-powered electronics. The use of MXenes in combination with other 2D NMs to self-assemble heterostructures and electronics from solution is the most intriguing prospect.

Defects (such as vacancies and ad-atoms), their development mechanism during synthesis and processing, and their impact on the stability and characteristics of MXenes are all relevant topics. Defect engineering will provide you greater power over the attributes. MXenes with enormous lateral dimensions are formed by selective etching of A layers from MAX phase single crystals (viz., V2AlC and V4AlC3) (hundreds of micrometers). More work is needed to generate massive single crystal MXenes, since their characterization would increase our understanding of MXene physical characteristics, perhaps leading to novel applications in the electronics sector. Direct evidence of B.G in semiconducting MXenes, for example, is still needed. Because vapor deposition technologies have not yet created SL MXenes, roll-to-roll synthesis of single crystal MXenes must be proved [69].

Although many research studies demonstrate the great potential of MXenes in biomedical applications, their scarce reproducibility, uncontrollable morphology, and broad size distributions render the limit of these materials in clinical applications. MXenes for translation applications confronts a number of unforeseen challenges, including scalable manufacture, accurate composition control, targeted surface engineering, and thorough long-standing biocompatibility/bio-safety evaluation. More study on toxicity of various MXene types in humans is needed. MXenes' effects on cytotoxicity and genotoxicity at the cellular level via the signaling pathway have received little attention. Additionally, before novel MXene NMs based devices for medical application are approved by the regulatory authorities, their potential toxicity should always be taken into account and handled The physicochemical characterization of MXenes carried out under the same experimental settings is more difficult due to the possibility that MXenes' properties may vary throughout biological testing. A rule containing certain essential elements to standardize the method of comparing characterization and findings should be suggested in light of this. Researchers have offered some minimal agreements for nanomaterial characterization, including the production process of materials and the biological test, as a result of some efforts done in this regard.

MXene may naturally enter through physiological barriers for instance the blood-air; testis, brain, placental, and skin barrier allow drugs to reach organs. On the other hand, such issues were not addressed in subsequent MXene toxicity study. Furthermore, the biopolymers encasing MXene's degradability must be assessed throughout time. There is presently a paucity of new information on MXene's interactions with many types of blood cells, hepatocytes, myocytes, and endothelial cells. Insights collected might help forecast whether MXene poses any dangers to connective tissues and organs. MXenes studied biocompatibility either in-vivo or in-vitro in lower order animal models for example, zebra fish and mice. More study on higher-order animals, for instance monkeys or dogs is advised. The bio-degradability and in vivo elimination of MXene residues following therapy had not been investigated, which is consistent with MXene's new usage in PTT and theranostic. MXene NPs have the potential to bio-accumulate in organs, which is a serious issue. The bulk of previous toxicity research focused on temporary toxic effects and gave little information on long-standing consequences. As a result, the MXene long-term toxicity should be explored further. As a result, MXene's long-term adverse consequences should be thoroughly investigated before it is used in human clinical trials. Furthermore, the therapeutic MXene has potential applications in other biomedical fields, including as stem-cell engineering, immunological treatment, tissue regeneration, genomics, biomedical engineering, and maybe new cancer therapies, should be explored and advanced. Large and diverse initiatives from biology, chemistry, physics, and engineering are expected to expand mechanistic knowledge of MXene-based nanomedicine systems and allow current effective paradigms to be employed more quickly and broadly in flexible biomedicine.

Even though there has been a lot of theoretical analysis and experimental study into the characterization of MXenes, but the road to a successful clinical application is still quite difficult and drawn out. To examine the systemic cytotoxicity (both short- and long-term), transport routes, and biological immunity of various MXenes with diverse sizes and modifications is important. For example, surface modifications and particle size play crucial roles in modifying the biological behaviors of MXenes NMs. The combined efforts of scientific researchers from transdisciplinary and industrial sectors are required for a further and more in-depth investigation of MXene knowledge and clinical transformation. In the near future, we anticipate that the advancement of nanotechnology will encourage other scientific and technical discoveries that will enable the unrestricted use of MXenes in numerous healthcare sectors.
